title: Bits In Bio Weekly - April 15th 2025
text: 📢 Highlights FDA Embraces AI, Human Organoids, and Digital Twins Over Traditional Animal Testing Baker Lab's New RFdiffusion2 Nails All 41 Test Cases in Enzyme Design Benchmark GenomOncology's Open-Source BioMCP Gives LLMs Real-Time Biomedical Data Access Not yet a member of our super awesome slack community of >8500? Join HERE 🤗 👀 In Case You Missed it … FDA Bids Farewell to Lab Animals, Embraces AI Models and Organoids In a landmark move, the FDA is modernizing drug development by phasing out animal testing requirements for monoclonal antibodies and other new drugs. In its place comes a toolkit of high-tech alternatives: AI-driven simulations and human-derived models (like organoids and organs-on-chips) that mimic human biology more directly than mice ever could. The FDA's animal testing phaseout initiative represents one of the first major policy shifts under Commissioner Martin Makary's leadership, with pharmaceutical companies soon leveraging computational models and organ-on-chip technologies to simulate how monoclonal antibodies distribute through the human body and predict potential toxicity issues. This regulatory evolution comes after the passage of the FDA Modernization Act 2.0 in 2022, which for the first time authorized using non-animal alternatives in preclinical testing but had seen limited industry adoption due to regulatory compliance concerns. The plan promises safer, faster drug development by using advanced computational toxicity predictions and lab-grown human tissues instead of live animal trials. Starting with new IND applications, the agency will encourage data from these cutting-edge methods and even lean on real-world human data from abroad to avoid redundant animal studies. Companies that provide strong non-animal safety evidence may get quicker reviews, hinting at cheaper R&D and cures arriving sooner. It’s a paradigm shift in regulatory science that could make drug testing more humane and efficient worldwide. Baker Lab's New AI Creates Custom Enzymes from Atomic Blueprints The Institute for Protein Design at University of Washington has released RFdiffusion2 , a breakthrough AI system that creates enzymes from minimal atomic specifications without requiring pre-defined rotamers or index positions, solving key limitations in existing enzyme design methods. The system builds on collaborative work from the Baker Lab and DiMaio Lab at IPD along with the Barzilay Lab and Jaakkola Lab at MIT, using deep learning to design protein structures precisely tuned for specific chemical reactions with unprecedented accuracy. In in-silico testing, RFdiffusion2 solved all 41 cases in a challenging enzyme design benchmark (the Atomic Motif Enzyme benchmark based on the M-CSA database), dramatically outperforming previous tools that solved only 16 cases. Laboratory validation confirmed the model's effectiveness by successfully producing active enzymes for five distinct chemical reactions with fewer than 100 designs tested per case—a stark departure from traditional workflows requiring screening of thousands of candidates—including retroaldolase, cysteine hydrolase, and zinc hydrolase designs, with the latter exhibiting orders-of-magnitude higher activity than previously engineered metallohydrolases. The innovation enables a broader range of active site geometries and unlocks new applications in enzyme design, potentially accelerating development of custom enzymes for applications like plastic degradation, drug manufacturing, and other complex chemical reactions. BioMCP Empowers LLMs with Standardized Access to Biomedical Data Ohio-based GenomOncology has announced their open-source project called BioMCP , which tackles a major AI blind spot by giving research assistants like GPT-style models real-time access to biomedical data. Launched by GenomOncology’s Ian Maurer, the BioMCP server hooks into public NIH APIs (ClinicalTrials.gov, PubMed/PubTator, MyVariant.info, etc.) so an AI can pull in up-to-date clinical trial info, papers, and genomic variant data as context. The idea is to prevent the “stale knowledge” problem — ensuring an AI helper in precision oncology always consults the latest evidence instead of just its fixed training data. A demo shows an AI querying trial criteria and genetic databases on the fly via BioMCP, hinting at more interactive, data-aware medical chatbots. Released under MIT open source license, you can find it here . Startup Launches with $121M to Wipe Out Disease-Causing Antibodies Newcomer Merida Bioscience s has emerged with a $121 million Series A to develop therapies for autoimmune and allergic diseases by literally removing the problem-causing antibodies. Merida’s platform creates engineered proteins (built on an IgG Fc scaffold) that latch onto only the bad antibodies – like those that drive Graves’ disease or severe allergies – and tag them for destruction by the body, while leaving normal immune function alone. The approach promises to be more precise and durable than today’s blunt immunosuppressants: imagine treating an autoimmune disease by knocking out just the harmful antibody (and the B-cells that produce it) instead of nuking the whole immune system. Their lead candidate targets Graves’ disease by neutralizing the rogue antibodies attacking the thyroid, potentially restoring healthy metabolism without the usual drastic measures. With backing from Third Rock, Bain, and others, Merida is betting that a cleaner fix to immune malfunctions is within reach – one that could tackle dozens of antibody-driven conditions that have long evaded selective treatment. PTM-Mamba: Enhancing Protein Language Modeling with Post-Translational Modification Integration Researchers have unveiled PTM-Mamba is a newly developed protein language model developed by at Duke University that addresses a critical gap in current protein modeling by explicitly representing post-translational modifications (PTMs), which vastly expand protein functional diversity and are often implicated in diseases. The model integrates PTM tokens using bidirectional Mamba blocks—a modern neural network architecture that efficiently processes sequential data in both forward and backward directions—fused with ESM-2 protein language model embeddings via a specially designed gating mechanism that maintains comprehension of both wild-type and modified protein sequences. The team utilized multiple protein language models including SaLT&PepPr , PepPrCLIP , and PepMLM to generate the targeting peptides. PTM-Mamba outperforms baseline models on several key tasks including disease association prediction, druggability assessment, and determining how PTMs affect protein-protein interactions. The model also shows promising capability for zero-shot PTM discovery, where it can predict plausible modifications for specific residues without additional training. This research has potentially significant applications in understanding disease mechanisms and improving therapeutic design with enhanced targeting specificity. The model is available on HuggingFace and code can be found on Github . IngeniX.AI Secures $9M to Simulate Clinical Trials Poland-based IngeniX.AI has raised $9 million to advance its multimodal AI foundation model for simulating clinical trials , potentially transforming how pharmaceutical companies evaluate drug candidates before committing to expensive human studies. The platform integrates diverse patient data—including genomics, proteomics, clinical records, and real-world evidence—to create virtual cohorts and simulate treatment responses across different populations, enabling researchers to rapidly iterate trial designs while identifying optimal endpoints and inclusion criteria. Founded by veterans from both AI development and pharmaceutical R&D, the company claims its simulations can predict clinical outcomes with up to 85% accuracy for certain therapeutic areas, potentially saving sponsors millions in development costs while reducing the ethical concerns associated with exposing patients to experimental therapies that might fail. The funding round was led by a mix of biotech-focused venture capital firms and strategic pharmaceutical partners, with plans to expand the platform's capabilities across additional therapeutic areas while pursuing validation studies comparing simulation predictions to actual trial outcomes. IngeniX.AI joins a growing field of computational trial simulation providers, though it distinguishes itself through its foundation model approach that continuously learns from new data inputs while optimizing for specific disease biology and drug mechanisms . Recursion's AI and Enamine Curates 15K Molecules for 100 Tough Drug Targets Salt Lake City Recursion Pharmaceuticals and Enamine have unveiled AI-curated screening libraries targeting 100 historically challenging drug targets , leveraging machine learning to identify novel chemical matter for proteins previously deemed "undruggable." The collaboration combines Recursion's ML-powered prediction platform with Enamine's billion-compound REAL (Readily Accessible) database to generate focused libraries of 3,000-5,000 compounds for each target, dramatically reducing screening inefficiencies compared to traditional high-throughput approaches. Among the targets are protein-protein interactions, transcription factors, RNA-binding proteins, and other challenging mechanisms that have resisted conventional drug discovery methods despite their therapeutic potential across multiple disease areas. Recursion's predictive models were reportedly trained on the company's proprietary biological data, including cellular morphology changes detected through high-content imaging across tens of millions of experiments. The libraries will be available to pharmaceutical partners seeking to jumpstart development programs against these difficult targets, potentially expanding the universe of druggable biology while accelerating the early discovery phase for multiple therapeutic areas. ImmunityBio Gets $75M Lifeline from New Investor A leading immunotherapy biotech, ImmunityBio, has secured a $75 million equity infusion from a single institutional investo r – a welcome lifeline for its pipeline of cancer and vaccine programs. The deal involves a direct stock sale (with warrants that could bring in an extra $90M later) to shore up working capital at a time when the company was rumored to be cash-strapped. It comes on the heels of ImmunityBio’s first product approval – an IL-15 based therapy called Anktiva for bladder cancer – and will help fund ongoing trials and manufacturing. Large injections of capital like this are often a sanity check for struggling biotechs, suggesting that at least one big backer sees promise in ImmunityBio’s approach to activating the immune system against disease. With the new funds, the company can keep pushing forward on its natural killer cell and T cell-based treatments instead of worrying about its runway. 23andMe’s Genetic Trove Pegged at $289M Amid Bankruptcy Proceedings A new analysis from data valuation firm Gulp Data puts a $289 million price tag on 23andMe’s genetic database just as the direct-to-consumer DNA company grapples with potential bankruptcy. 23andMe has millions of customers’ DNA data paired with health information – a goldmine for pharmaceutical research if it were ever sold – though privacy concerns loom large. Gulp Data arrived at the figure using market comps and proprietary algorithms, underscoring how valuable big biomedical datasets can be even when a company’s finances falter. The situation spotlights a thorny question: if 23andMe goes under, could its trove be treated like an asset to be bought, and what would that mean for customers who consented to give their DNA? At the very least, this valuation is a reminder that in biotech, data itself is often the crown jewel – something investors (and regulators) are watching as much as any drug or device. Recursion's AI-Designed Molecule Reaches Human Testing In Record Time Recursion Pharmaceuticals has dosed the first patient in a Phase 1 trial of REC-2554 , an AI-designed MALT1 (Mucosa-Associated Lymphoid Tissue Lymphoma Translocation Protein 1) inhibitor targeting both B-cell malignancies and autoimmune disorders. The small molecule, discovered using the company's Recursion OS platform and massive biological dataset, represents a significant milestone for AI-driven drug discovery by successfully translating computational predictions into clinical-stage development for a valuable oncology target. MALT1 functions as both a scaffold protein and a protease in the NF-κB signaling pathway, making it a compelling but complex target for intervention in conditions with immune dysregulation, with preclinical data showing REC-2554 has potent anti-tumor activity and favorable pharmacokinetic properties. Now in a Phase 1 study (dubbed EXCELERIZE ), researchers will evaluate its safety, optimal dose, and initial efficacy both alone and later in combination with other treatments. It’s a milestone for AI-driven drug discovery: a computationally-designed cancer drug advancing in the clinic, potentially faster and more precisely tailored than traditional chemistry could manage.The open-label Phase 1 trial will evaluate the safety, tolerability, pharmacokinetics, and preliminary efficacy of escalating doses in patients with relapsed or refractory B-cell malignancies, with plans to expand into autoimmune indications following initial safety validation. 🗓️ Upcoming BiB Events See our handy dandy Lu.ma event calendar HERE , please RSVP so folks can plan accordingly! .Bits in Bio April Journal Club - Drug Repositioning , Wednesday, April 23 at 5:30 PM PT Thank you to Steve Cheney for organizing! title: Bits In Bio Weekly - April 8th 2025
text: 📢 Highlights 34M PubMed Abstracts and 40 Databases Yields iKraph The Largest Biomedical Knowledge Graph Yet Siemens Bets $5.1B That Scientific Software Enhanced Manufacturing, Aquiring Dotmatics U of Michigan Achieve 75% Power Reduction for Robotics Control Systems with Biomimetic Computing Architecture Not yet a member of our super awesome slack community of >9000? Join HERE 🤗 👀 In Case You Missed it … AI Crawled 34 Million PubMed to Build The Biggest Biomedical Knowledge Graph Yet In a new Nature Machine Intelligence paper (also here ) researchers from Florida State University and AI company Insilicom have unveiled iKraph, a massive biomedical knowledge graph built by processing over 34 million PubMed abstracts with their award-winning natural language processing pipeline. The system extracts nearly 31 million unique relations between 10.7 million biological entities, far exceeding the content in manually curated databases. The team developed a probabilistic semantic reasoning algorithm that enables causal inference between indirectly connected entities, demonstrating its effectiveness in drug repurposing for COVID-19 where one-third of their predicted candidates from early 2020 were later validated by clinical trials or research publications. This potent resource additionally integrates relations from 40 public databases and high-throughput genomics data. What is impressive is that it was trained on only abstracts, and not the body of the articles itself. Its code is available on Github . Behind the Deal: Siemens Takes a Big Step with Dotmatics German industrial conglomerate Siemens’ decision to acquire Dotmatics for $5.1 billion is a bold move into the life sciences software arena. The sales process has been public for some time , but the final deal details show positive trends for the life sciences industry. This acquisition isn't just about numbers; it reflects a deeper transformation. Dotmatics started in 2005 as a spin-off from MSK, initially serving as a specialized data platform for researchers. Over the years, it has rapidly expanded through strategic acquisitions, including BioBright in 2020, which focused on lab automation, and merging with Insightful Science in 2021, known for popular tools like GraphPad Prism and SnapGene.By 2022, Dotmatics combined its various offerings under a single brand, catering to over 10,000 clients and two million scientists globally. Its latest product, Luma, a low-code platform that integrates complex scientific data, further positions it in the realm of AI-driven research and development—a space Siemens is eager to dominate. Siemens’ strategy is straightforward: they aim to integrate Dotmatics into their Xcelerator platform , creating a seamless digital journey from discovery to manufacturing in life sciences. The potential market is significant—an estimated $11 billion—but the acquisition cost is also steep. The 17x forward revenue / 39x EBITDA multiple has raised eyebrows, especially when considering strong competitors like Dassault Systèmes and Thermo Fisher.This move is far from a standard software acquisition. It represents a calculated risk to spearhead the digital evolution of R&D. Siemens stands to gain a comprehensive platform and a robust scientific user community. However, making this integration work and meeting high synergy expectations will be the true challenge. Neuromorphic Computing Dramatically Cuts Energy Needs for Robotics University of Michigan researchers have developed a brain-inspired computer that can control a rolling robot while using just 25% of the power required by conventional controllers. The system combines a neuromorphic processor with a novel algorithm that mimics how neurons process information—using sparse, event-driven computations rather than continuous sensing. The approach allows the robot to navigate obstacles while consuming a mere 200 milliwatts of power, comparable to a single LED bulb. As robotics applications expand in healthcare and biotechnology, this energy efficiency breakthrough could enable longer-running autonomous systems for everything from laboratory automation to therapeutic delivery. UniteLabs Secures €2.77M to Create the Operating System for Lab Automation Munich-Based UniteLabs has raised €2.77 million in pre-seed funding to develop what they're describing as "the operating system for AI-driven biotech research." The Munich-based startup, founded by Robert Zechlin, Lukas Bromig, and Julian Willand, is tackling a fundamental challenge in laboratory automation: the lack of standardized connectivity between different instruments. Their platform creates bidirectional connectors that allow diverse lab equipment to communicate seamlessly, breaking down data silos that have historically limited automation potential. The round was led by NAP (formerly Cavalry Ventures) with participation from PUSH, Acurio Ventures, OMA Business Angels, and LANA Ventures. UniteLabs has already built 40 connectors for various lab devices and is initially focusing on liquid handlers—critical automation tools used in experiments ranging from COVID-19 testing to DNA sequencing. Their longer-term vision involves expanding to other instruments including chromatography systems, plate readers, robotic arms, and bioreactors, ultimately creating the technological foundation for AI integration into laboratory processes. Mapping tissues like ecosystems Researchers from Stanford, MIT, and Weill Cornell have unveiled MESA (Multiomics and Ecological Spatial Analysis), a framework that blends spatial omics, single-cell data integration, and concepts from biodiversity to decode tissue architecture quantitatively. Instead of relying on predefined cell types, MESA defines cellular neighborhoods using protein and mRNA expression, then quantifies spatial heterogeneity using diversity indices inspired by ecology. Applied to human tonsil, mouse spleen, and liver cancer datasets, MESA reveals conserved and functionally distinct microenvironments and outperforms existing methods in linking tissue structure to disease progression. The tool is open-source and available as a Python package on GitHub . Neuromorphic Computing Dramatically Cuts Energy Needs for Robotics University of Michigan researchers have developed a brain-inspired computer that can control a rolling robot while using just 25% of the power required by conventional controllers. The system combines a neuromorphic processor with a novel algorithm that mimics how neurons process information—using sparse, event-driven computations rather than continuous sensing. The approach allows the robot to navigate obstacles while consuming a mere 200 milliwatts of power, comparable to a single LED bulb. As robotics applications expand in healthcare and biotechnology, this energy efficiency breakthrough could enable longer-running autonomous systems for everything from laboratory automation to therapeutic delivery. Ginkgo Join Forces with Plex Research for AI-Powered Drug Discovery Plex Research, an AI-driven drug discovery startup, is teaming up with Ginkgo Bioworks’ data arm to probe their gene expression dataset . Calle GDPx2, the dataset is a large-scale transcriptomics survey that measures compound-induced gene expression across diverse human cells. At close to 4 terabytes (TB) in size, it consists of data generated from 4 human primary cell types, 85 compound treatments, 6 doses and 4 replicates.Their AI platform will hunt for hidden links between drugs and diseases – think drug repurposing leads or previously unknown mechanisms of action – by analyzing how diverse compounds perturb various human cell types. It’s an example of how combining high-throughput perturbation profiling data with novel AI methods can illuminate new biology from old experiments, potentially accelerating drug discovery in a data-driven way. AI Capital's Mega-Fundraise Positions It for Biotech's Computational Future In true AI fashion the curiously titled VC firm “AI Capital” has secured an eye-popping $2 billion for its second fund focused on computational biology and AI-driven healthcare companies. The raise, one of the largest ever for a biotech-focused fund, comes as AI-native drug discovery companies like Isomorphic Labs and Insilico Medicine demonstrate increasing clinical validation. Beyond just backing startups, AI Capital plans to launch its own ventures and provide computational infrastructure for portfolio companies—potentially addressing the capital-intensive nature of biotech R&D. With big pharma increasingly embracing AI for drug discovery, the timing suggests we're reaching an inflection point where computational approaches are becoming central, not peripheral, to therapeutic development. Paige’s pan-cancer AI pathology tool earns FDA “Breakthrough” Designation Pathology AI pioneer Paige has been awarded an FDA Breakthrough Device designation for its new PanCancer Detect system. The tool uses machine learning to flag suspicious cancer foci across a wide range of tissue types (not just one organ) – reportedly the first AI in pathology to earn a multi-tissue designation. Trained on over 5 million pathology images, PanCancer Detect is part of Paige’s “Alba” platform and currently for research use only, but the Breakthrough status should fast-track its journey to the clinic. Paige also holds FDA clearances on single-tissue digital pathology tools for clinical tools such as Paige Prostate Detect and Paige FullFocus. It’s another signal that regulators are embracing AI diagnostics to help overworked pathologists catch more cancers, faster. Science Exchange Expands Lab Management Offerings with HappiLabs Acquisition Scientific outsourcing website Science Exchange has acquired virtual lab management company HappiLabs in a move to streamline R&D operations for biotech companies. The acquisition adds HappiLabs' procurement and vendor management capabilities to Science Exchange's existing marketplace of over 3,500 CROs and service providers. Together, they'll offer an integrated solution for outsourced research, lab supplies procurement, and vendor management—all aimed at helping biotechs maximize their runway in today's challenging funding environment. Inverting prediction for general binder design Born from a collaboration between Swiss and MIT researchers, t he new protein AI tool BoltzDesign1 introduces a structure-based protein design framework that inverts the Boltz-1 all-atom structure prediction model to generate protein binders across various targets, including small molecules, nucleic acids, and metals.. Developed by researchers at MIT and EPFL, the method avoids the computational cost of backpropagation through diffusion steps by optimizing directly on the predicted distogram. This enables flexible ligand modeling and generalizable binder design without fine-tuning. Compared to RfDiffusionAA, BoltzDesign1 produces more diverse structures and achieves higher in silico success rates across multiple metrics. The code and data are available on GitHub . Differential Bio Exits Stealth Mode with Bold Biomanufacturing Vision and $2M A new AI-powered biomanufacturing startup, Differential Bio, has emerged from stealth with $2M in seed funding to transform how we produce biologic s. Differential Bio, founded by former NASA scientists, combines AI optimization with automated robotic systems to create what they're calling "self-driving bioreactors." Their platform aims to slash development timelines from years to months while reducing production costs by up to 90%. Rather than focusing solely on R&D, the company is tackling the entire biomanufacturing workflow—from cell line development through scale-up. With pharmaceutical manufacturing costs skyrocketing, their timing couldn't be better. 🗓️ Upcoming BiB Events See our handy dandy Lu.ma event calendar HERE , please RSVP so folks can plan accordingly! . title: Bits In Bio Weekly - April 1st 2025
text: 📢 Highlights Google-Backed Isomorphic Labs Scores $600M to Push AI Drug Programs to Clinic Inside Pfizer's Option-Based Flagship Alliance: Innovation Outsourcing or Pipeline Padding? Bioptimus Releases Open-Source Pathology Model H-Optimus-1 that Outperforms Competition in Benchmarks Not yet a member of our super awesome slack community of >8500? Join HERE 🤗 👀 In Case You Missed it … Company Behind AlphaFold Raises $600M to Advance AI Drug Design and Internal Pipeline Isomorphic Labs, the AI drug discovery firm spun out of Google's DeepMind in 2021, has raised $600 million in its first external funding round led by Thrive Capital with ample participation from Google Ventures and Alphabet (who spun them out). The company was launched to advance human health by building on the Nobel-winning AlphaFold system, and has since developed powerful AI models serving scientific discovery, including AlphaFold 3, which can predict (albeit with varying degrees of success) the structure of proteins, DNA, RNA, and ligands and how they interact. According to CEO Demis Hassabis, who also leads DeepMind, the funding will "further turbocharge the development of our next-generation AI drug design engine, help us advance our own programs into clinical development, and is a significant step forward span our mission of one day solving all disease with the help of AI." Isomorphic programs across multiple therapeutic areas and drug modalities, with a focus on oncology and immunology for its internal programs, while its external partnerships include strategic research collaborations with Eli Lilly ($45 million upfront, up to $1.7 billion in milestones) and Novartis ($37.5 million upfront, up to $1.2 billion in milestones). Behind the Deal: How Pfizer’s option-based R&D strategy with Flagship reflects a shift towards low-risk pipeline expansion Pfizer’s sixth collaboration with Flagship Pioneering —this time targeting autoimmune diseases—reveals a high-stakes gamble on outsourcing innovation while sidestepping traditional M&A risks. The partnership’s evolution since 2023 underscores Pfizer’s strategic pivot to leverage Flagship’s “bioplatform ecosystem” as a substitute for internal R&D, with a $50M upfront/$700M-per-program milestone structure allowing selective acquisitions of validated candidates. This model lets Pfizer access 40+ Flagship startups while avoiding costly buyouts —a calculated response to its post-COVID pipeline pressures. The autoimmune focus reflects commercial opportunism: existing therapies like J&J’s Stelara ($10B annual sales) face patent cliffs, creating a $200B market void by 2030. Yet critics argue the approach prioritizes portfolio volume over therapeutic boldness—the partnership’s first obesity program with ProFound Therapeutics repurposes platform assets rather than pursuing novel mechanisms. While Flagship touts “de-risked” innovation, Pfizer’s option-based model transfers early-stage failure costs to partners, raising questions about the long-term viability. For Flagship, the deal validates its role as a pharma R&D contractor, but dependency on Pfizer’s whims introduces fragility: the agreement lets Pfizer abandon programs preclinically with minimal penalty. As rivals like Novo Nordisk deepen Flagship ties , this collaboration represents Big Pharma’s shift from blockbuster bets to portfolio spread strategies—a low-commitment tactic that may yield incremental gains but risks missing transformative therapies. H-Optimus-1, Fully Open Source Pathology Foundation Model, Released by Bioptimus French startup Bioptimus has launched H-optimus-1, the world's largest open-source AI foundation model for pathology , with 1.1 billion parameters and trained on a proprietary dataset of several hundred million images from over 500,000 histopathology slides across 4,000 clinical practices. The model achieves state-of-the-art performance in several critical medical diagnostic tasks and has been evaluated on both tile-level tasks (identifying tissue types/characteristics) and slide-level tasks (detecting biomarkers or metastasis). According to co-founder and CEO of Bioptimus, Professor Jean-Philippe Vert, it is just one in a long series of models" they will create, with future models incorporating additional pathology images from Europe, Asia, and Africa as well as other modalities like genomics and proteomics. Since its release in July 2024, the H-Optimus model series has outperformed all other pathology models in independent benchmarks, including assessments conducted by Harvard Medical School's HEST program and the University of Leeds. Available on Hugging Face . OpenFold3 will train on proprietary data from AbbVie and J&J in secure environments AlphaFold has revolutionized structural biology and drug design by leveraging publicly available protein structures from the PDB. However, predicting protein-drug interactions remains challenging due to the limited data in public databases.To help address this, the AI Structural Biology (AISB) Consortium announced a new training initiative where OpenFold3, a protein structure prediction system developed by Mohammed AlQuraishi's lab at Columbia University, will be fine-tuned using proprietary data from AbbVie and Johnson & Johnson in a confidentiality-preserving and secure federated environment powered by Apheris . Consortium members will have first access to the model, with hopes of attracting more pharmaceutical companies to contribute data. While widespread availability may take time, this initiative will provide valuable insights into whether increased data improves predictive performance. More details on the data scarcity problem facing can be found here . First DREAM Target 2035 Drug Discovery Challenge on Synapse.org The inaugural DREAM Target 2035 Drug Discovery Challenge has been launched. The competition invites data scientists to build machine learning models based on DNA encoded libraries (DEL) data to discover bioactive drug-like molecules for WDR91, a target protein, using an approach introduced in 2020 by Google and X-Chem. The challenge has two stages: first, participants train ML models on a library of 330K compounds (30,000 DEL hit candidates and related inactives) to retrospectively identify 145 confirmed hits hidden in an unrelated chemical library of ~370,000 diverse molecules, with teams ranked based on the chemical diversity of true positives in their top 50 predictions. The top 25 teams that predict true positives with at least two different chemical scaffolds advance to stage two, where they predict up to 50 hits from Enamine's collection of 4.4 million compounds, which will be experimentally tested at the Structural Genomics Consortium (SGC). This open science challenge supports Target 2035, a global movement focused on creating chemical and biological tools to study human proteins and inform drug discovery, with the winning team receiving a $5,000 cash prize, and participants who join MAINFRAME (a community of data scientists committed to open-source models) receiving additional benefits like waived experimental testing costs. The challenge runs from the end of April 2025 to June 2025, with all data shared publicly and no patents filed, and the top three ML models being made available on the AIRCHECK platform. Aqemia Secures $7.4M for Physics-based AI for RNA Targeting Paris and London-based Aqemia has announced securing an additional $7.4M for broadening and accelerating its computational platform to target RNAs using its physics-enabled generative AI engine, building on successes in epitranscriptomics to develop novel small-molecule drugs against this emerging class of therapeutic targets. The new funding brings Aqemia’s total capital to $125.4M, though the valuation remains undisclosed. What distinguishes Aqemia from other AI drug discovery platforms is its combination of quantum-inspired physics algorithms and generative AI, which generates its own data from scratch using physics-based calculations rather than relying on existing experimental data, making it as much as 10,000 times faster than contemporary methods. The company's model is trained on the laws of physics that govern how molecules are built in nature and when prompted to create a molecule for a specific target, it "invents" millions of possible candidates which are then tested using quantum-inspired algorithms, with feedback loops continuously improving the model's efficiency. Transcend Reports Positive Phase 2 Trial Results for MDMA Analog for PTSD Treatment New York based Transcend Therapeutics has announced successful Phase 2 trial results for its MDMA analog TSND-201 (methylone), showing significant improvements in post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) symptoms that began on Day 8 and persisted through Day 64 of the trial. Unlike traditional MDMA-assisted therapy approaches, Transcend's treatment doesn't require psychotherapy sessions as the molecule is characterized as a non-hallucinogenic triple reuptake inhibitor and releaser that acts on serotonin, norepinephrine, and dopamine, with patients receiving the drug orally once a week for four weeks. By Day 64, scores on the CAPS-5 PTSD scale had fallen by 23.28 points in the TSND-201 arm compared to 13.64 in the placebo group, achieving the primary endpoint with statistically significant improvements over placebo at every assessment point starting from Day 8. The speed and depth of response appears competitive with other PTSD treatments, including Lykos Therapeutics' MDMA therapy which was submitted for FDA approval after showing a 23.7-point drop in CAPS-5 scores after 18 weeks (versus Transcend's comparable results in just 9 weeks). Transcend, which closed a $40 million series A round in 2023 and has raised around $50 million in total, is continuing another Phase 2 trial that includes an open-label portion and a randomized section testing different dosages, with enough funding to reach the phase 2 readout expected in 2025. 🗓️ Upcoming BiB Events See our handy dandy Lu.ma event calendar HERE , please RSVP so folks can plan accordingly! . title: BiB Celebrates Women's History Month - Part 2
text: As part of Women’s History Month, Bits in Bio has gathered inspirational stories from women leaders within our community. We’re excited to share these stories with our members and the broader ecosystem, highlighting their unique journeys, lessons, challenges, and achievements. As we wrap up Women’s History Month, we are delighted to feature Holly Uber from How Women Lead. Holly is a remarkable digital health leader, investor, advisor, and board member. Her story has taught us valuable lessons, moved us deeply, and inspired us. We invite you to celebrate glimpses of her journey with us below. The Power of Perspective: Evolving Beyond a Single Career Path Like many graduates, I once felt the pressure to define my career path. "What do you want to be when you grow up?" was the ubiquitous question that haunted me. But as I've discovered, life rarely follows a linear trajectory. Embracing the Unexpected Journey My career began in the Peace Corps, where I engaged in agricultural work and unexpectedly found myself assisting in local schools. This experience ignited a passion for education, leading me to pursue a master's degree in teaching secondary science. Even then, I knew the classroom wouldn't be my final destination. I taught various subjects in different schools, each experience adding a new layer to my professional toolkit. When I moved to the Bay Area, I saw an opportunity to pivot once again. Recognizing the disconnect between tech products and classroom needs, I decided to bridge that gap by working on the product side of educational technology. My journey took another unexpected turn when I received a call to bring my skills to the digital health sector. This led to an exciting opportunity to work on Software-as-a-Medical Device (SaMD), further expanding my expertise and allowing me to contribute to innovative healthcare solutions. This series of transitions exemplifies the power of embracing change and leveraging diverse experiences to create a unique and adaptable career path. The Art of the Pivot: Lessons Learned My journey taught me valuable lessons about career transitions: Understand your value: Identify the unique skills and perspectives you've gained from each experience. Articulate your worth: Learn to communicate your value in a way that resonates with your target industry. Master the lingo: Immerse yourself in the language and culture of the field you want to enter. Breaking Down Silos: Driving Innovation Through Cross-Disciplinary Thinking True innovation often occurs at the intersection of different disciplines. My diverse career has enabled me to: Cross-pollinate ideas: Leverage strategies and insights from various sectors to create unique solutions. Identify novel approaches: Apply principles learned across different fields to tackle complex challenges. Develop comprehensive understanding: Build a holistic view of problems, leading to more effective strategies. The Dual Perspective Advantage: Balancing Strategic Vision with Tactical Execution My ability to navigate between strategic vision and tactical execution has been crucial. This dual perspective allows me to: Dive deep ("tree" view): Understand the specific nuances and intricacies of challenges. See the bigger picture ("forest" view): Grasp how individual elements interconnect and impact the overall strategy. Embracing a Multi-Faceted Identity: Building a Dynamic and Adaptable Career Discovering the concept of a "portfolio career" was a turning point. I actively embrace this approach by: Engaging in multiple areas of interest: Pursuing diverse projects, looking for places to volunteer, invest and advise start-ups. Adapting readily to market changes: Quickly pivoting and acquiring new skills to stay ahead of the curve. Creating a unique professional identity: Building a personal brand that reflects my diverse experiences and expertise. This approach has led to increased professional fulfillment and a broader impact across various domains. It's also fostered a diverse and supportive professional network. Looking Forward: The Power of Diverse Perspectives As I continue to evolve, I'm excited to connect seemingly unrelated experiences and collaborate with individuals who value diverse perspectives. My journey has demonstrated that professional identities can be as dynamic and multifaceted as we allow them to be. I am always seeking new opportunities to leverage my skills and experiences to contribute to innovative and impactful projects. In this ever-changing world, the ability to adapt, learn, and bring diverse perspectives to the table is not just an asset – it's a necessity. Thanks for reading! title: Bits In Bio Weekly - March 25th 2025
text: 📢 Highlights Google's TxGemma Joins a Crowded AI drug Discovery Race Sanofi acquires Immunoscience Specialist Dren Bio and Their Early Stage Antibody For $1.9B deal 23&Me Starts Its Final Chapter As It Files For Bankruptcy and Its CEO Resigns Not yet a member of our super awesome slack community of >8500? Join HERE 🤗 👀 In Case You Missed it … Open-Source Pharma - Google Debuts TxGemma for Drug Discovery Google announced a n ew collection of AI models for drug discovery, called TxGemma, emphasizing an “open” approach to accelerate early R&D​. Set to be released via Google’s Health AI Developer Foundations program, the models can interpret both natural language and diverse molecular structures. Researchers will be able to ask TxGemma questions to predict properties of potential therapies (e.g. safety or efficacy). Google hasn’t confirmed if the models will be fully open-source or commercially usable. This initiative joins a crowded AI drug discovery field – over 460 startups have attracted ~$60B in investment – where even Google’s own spinout Isomorphic Labs and others have faced recent challenges translating AI breakthroughs into clinical success. The platform play comes late to the party, with offerings such as Nvidia's BioNemo , which announced several new models/features this past week at the companies annual technical conference, having an existing install base with years of user validation across the industry. Behind the Deal: Sanofi’s $1.9B Immunology Bet on Dren Bio – More Than Meets the Eye Sanofi’s $1.9B deal for Dren Bio’s DR-0201 reveals fascinating insights beyond the headlines . Founded in 2019, in just six years, the company catapulted from startup to billion-dollar acquisition target, exemplifying the biotech industry’s breakneck pace.This acquisition follows Sanofi’s broader strategy to become an “immunoscience powerhouse”. Sanofi isn’t necessarily chasing the flashiest, most cutting-edge platforms. Instead, they’re strategically acquiring assets like DR-0201, which, while still in relatively early clinical stages, offer a precise, differentiated mechanism – deep B-cell depletion – and a defined path to market.The deal also highlights a renewed interest in deep B-cell depletion, mirroring the excitement surrounding CAR-T therapies in oncology, where the goal is a “one-and-done” treatment. Dren Bio’s success story showcases how small biotechs can create substantial value and attract big pharma interest, challenging the David vs. Goliath narrative in the industry. The transaction structure, with $1.3B tied to potential milestones, aligns interests while providing Sanofi with downside protection, despite a hefty $600 million upfront payment. This transaction is more than just an acquisition; it’s a strategic move within Sanofi’s larger plan to dominate the immunology space. 23andMe files for bankruptcy as DNA testing demand wanes The embattled consumer DNA testing company 23andMe has filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy and initiated a search for a buyer. According to court filings, the company listed $277 million in assets vs. $215 million in liabilities, and secured $35 million in debtor-in-possession financing to stay operational during restructuring. The move comes just weeks after co-founder/CEO Anne Wojcicki’s offer to take the company private was summarily rejected – Wojcicki has since resigned as CEO, while keeping her board seat, and signaled in a recent Linkdin post her own ongoing interest in acquiring the assets of the company personally. She acknowledged the company’s challenges but noted its achievements since 2006 - over 15 million customers, 85% of whom consented to research; a drug-discovery partnership with GSK that yielded 50 programs; 250+ research papers. The bankruptcy follows a turbulent 2023–24: a five-month data breach exposed ~7 million customers’ data, laid off 200 of staff, and halted all therapeutics R&D despite a seeing a partial tumor response in a Phase 2 trial of the associated anti-CD200R1 antibody. 23andMe recently pivoted to telehealth and selling compounded GLP-1 weight-loss prescriptions via Lemonaid Health, as core DNA kit demand waned. The firm went public via SPAC in 2021 at a $3.5 billion valuation, but now trades at less than 0.70 USD a share as of the publishing, representing a >99% loss of value in the last 5 years. Roivant spinout VantAI debuts Neo-1 for structure prediction & generation VantAI, the NY-based Roivant spinout founded in 2019, has debuted “Neo-1”, an AI model that combines biomolecule structure prediction with de novo molecule generation in one framewor k. Revealed at NVIDIA GTC 2025, Neo-1 is described by the team that developed it as the “next step after AlphaFold 3,” capable of simultaneously predicting protein–small molecule interactions and designing novel compounds. The model was trained on massive structural datasets using hundreds of H100 GPUs and leverages a proprietary “NeoLink” dataset (cross-linking mass spec data). Neo-1 can generate complex drug-like molecules (e.g. molecular glues, bifunctional degraders) and co-fold proteins with ligands, potentially hitting previously undruggable targets. It’s already deployed in partnerships with Janssen, Blueprint Medicines, and BMS, as well as a collaboration with Boehringer Ingelheim, to accelerate discovery pipelines. Lilly-backed Ampersand nets $65M for AI-driven immunology drug discovery The Flagship Pioneering company, Ampersand Biomedicines, has raised $65 million in Series B funding to advance its preclinical pipeline in immunology and its AI-driven drug design platform. The raise is the first that the Eli Lilly backed startup outside capital since launching with $50M from Flagship in 2023, and is slated to bankroll the completion of preclinical studies for two “programmable” therapeutics targeting inflammatory disease and cancer respectively. Ampersand’s platform integrates machine learning with large molecular datasets to design improved versions of known drugs (“parent molecules”) that are more potent and have fewer off-target effects. Its approach, called “Address, Navigate, Design,” computationally maps ideal delivery targets in the body using multi-omics data so that new molecules act only at the disease site with minimal collateral effects. In practice, the company iterates in silico through many modifications to engineer drug candidates with better tissue targeting, then validates them experimentally. Ampersand is rapidly expanding: in 2024 it acquired AbCheck (an antibody discovery firm) and partnered with Pfizer to develop a targeted obesity drug, showing the broad applications of its technology. Lilly’s strategic investment suggests interest in Ampersand’s approach to in silico drug discovery and targeted delivery, though its CEO declined to comment on any specific partnership plans. BrainGPT Generates CT/Radiology Reports Indistinguishable from Human Clinicians In their recent publication in Nature, Stanford researchers have announced the development of BrainGPT, a multimodal large language model (LLM) that analyzes brain CT scans and generates radiology reports . To train the model, they curated a large text-image dataset consisting of 18,885 pairs and introduced new clinically relevant evaluation metrics for radiology report assessment. BrainGPT effectively captured key radiological details across various aspects, achieving an F1 score of 0.53 to 0.64. Additionally, when 11 physicians were asked to determine whether reports were generated by BrainGPT or written by humans based on the corresponding CT images, 56% of BrainGPT-generated captions were mistakenly identified as human-written. In other words, functionally indistinguishable. Transformer-based text-to-protein AI Model, MP4, from Bay Area Startup 310.ai A research collaboration between researchers at UC Berkeley and McGill University, and the Bio AI startup 310.ai, has developed a transformer-based AI model called MP4 that claims to generate novel, functional proteins from simple text descriptions . MP4 takes as input a detailed text prompt that includes fitness (desired functional and performance metrics), source organism or biological context, and sequence properties (including partial sequences, motifs, or structural hints). The input prompt is then processed through a text2feature preprocessing unit, which tokenizes it into a structured feature representation before the model outputs a complete protein sequence. Trained on 138,000 tokens and 3.2 billion unique data points, MP4 demonstrated impressive experimental results, with 84% of tested designs successfully expressed as stable proteins. Unlike previous models focused on specific tasks, MP4 offers a versatile approach that addresses both structural and functional objectives simultaneously. As the authors note in the paper, "While the current vocabulary understood by MP4 is constrained, future iterations will incorporate an expanded, precise, and technically sophisticated lexicon that will enable true molecular programming, where users can specify target protein properties—function, stability, binding affinity, and more—with fine-grained control." ReactWise raises $3.4M to automate drug manufacturing with AI and robotics UK-based and Y Combinator backed startup ReactWise has emerged with $3.4 million in pre-seed funding to develop AI-driven robotic manufacturing laboratories for diverse chemical and pharmaceuticals. The round included $500k from YC, a £1.2 million Innovate UK grant (~$1.6M), and $1.5M from venture investors. ReactWise’s platform uses machine learning to speed up chemical process development – allegedly up to 30× faster than traditional trial-and-error. The AI models are trained on data from high-throughput reaction experiments, with the team having run 300+ reactions in parallel so far, aiming for 20,000 reaction data points by mid-2025. The goal is “one-shot” optimization, where the AI can identify the best process parameters without multiple iterations, which the founders expect to achieve within two years. Building on five years of academic research at the University of Cambridge, the company was launched in 2024 by Dr. Alexander Pomberger and Dr. Daniel Wight and is running 12 pilot projects with major pharma companies and plans to transition its first pilots into paid subscriptions later this year. Spectral AI’s DeepView System Demonstrates Enhanced Capability in Assessing Burn Tissue In one of largest burn studies in the history of the United States, Spectral AI has demonstrated their AI-driven DeepView® System, which utilizes multispectral imaging and computer vision to predict burn healing potential, comparative superiority to human clinicians. In the Burn Validation Study, the system demonstrated significantly enhanced sensitivity (86.6%) in identifying non-healing tissue compared to physicians (40.8%). The system is a innocuous non-invasive, cart-based tool that is mobile, allowing clinicians to perform immediate, objective assessments of burn patients regardless of their skill level. The company plans to file for FDA approval in mid-2025 , with a potential product launch in 2026. Baker Lab Provides De Novo Designs Against GPCR’s The Institute of Protein Design's Baker lab has published a preprint describing the design of agonists and antagonists for G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs). GPCR’s have been implicated in numerous diseases, including cancer, cardiovascular, and metabolic disorders. The paper is replete with cutting edge method developments. To overcome the challenge of generating GPCR agonists, the team developed a "motif-directed RFDiffusion approach" that targets hot spot residues within the recessed binding pocket. The researchers also created MetaGen, an approach that utilizes scaffolds from the AlphaFold-generated metaproteome in Rosetta RifDock calculations. Lastly, the team developed a high-throughput screening platform called Receptor Diversion (RD), which operates directly in human cells and converts protein-protein interactions to optical phenotypes. The team successfully applied these methods to generate MRGPRX1 agonists and CXCR4, GLP1R, GIPR, GCGR, and CGRPR antagonists, with cryo-electron microscopy data confirming atomic-level agreement between designed and experimentally determined structures. Proscia lands $50M to scale its AI pathology platform The Philadelphia-based digital pathology software company, Proscia, has raised $50 million in new funding in a round led by Insight Partners, bringing its total ingested capital to $130M. Proscia will use the capital to expand capabilities in its flagship platform Concentriq , which helps digitize microscope-based pathology workflows. Concentriq is already widely adopted – 16 of the top 20 pharma companies use it , and it facilitates diagnosis for 22,000 patients per day – supporting both drug R&D and clinical diagnostics by enabling AI-powered slide analysis and data sharing. The company plans to enhance Concentriq with features like foundation-model embeddings to improve biomarker discovery and automate more pathology tasks. The funding will also go toward growing Proscia’s customer base and strengthening partnerships to drive industry-wide adoption of digital pathology. This push comes as pathology labs face a surge in cancer cases but a shrinking workforce. 🗓️ Upcoming BiB Events See our handy dandy Lu.ma event calendar HERE , please RSVP so folks can plan accordingly! Bits in Bio x BioLabs: San Diego Mixer , Thursday, March 27 at 6:00 PM, at BioLabs San Diego Thanks to Robbie Matthews, Diya Mohan, and Danielle Orozco Cosio for organizing! A BiB Afternoon at MIT Museum , Saturday, March 29 at 2:00 PM Thank you to Nelly Tian for organizing! London Bio Breakfast with Bits in Bio x ScienceMachine x future.bio , Wednesday, April 2 Thanks to Lorenzo Sani, Isabel Zhang, Jamie Tomalin, Benjamin Tenmann, Ines Ullmo, and Jonty for organizing! Minneapolis-St Paul Get Together , Wednesday, April 2 at 5:00 PM, at Fair State Brewing Cooperative Thanks to Brian Repko for organizing! Bits in Bio Philadelphia April Happy Hour , Friday, April 4 at 5:00 PM, at Landmark Americana University City Thank you to organizer AJ Adejare! 📰 Top Community Conversations We're excited to share part 1 of our series celebrating women's history month! Hear about the empowering realization of Karmen Čondić-Jurkić, Executive Director & Co-Founder, Open Molecular Software Foundation, that she can “just do things", as well as Savitha Sangameswaran's , Senior Research Scientist at Sage Bionetworks, lessons in resilience and self-compassion as she balanced academia, motherhood, and the unpredictable challenges of a global pandemic in her graduate studies. title: BiB Celebrates Women's History Month - Part 1
text: As part of Women’s History Month, Bits in Bio has gathered inspirational stories from women leaders within our community. We’re excited to share these stories with our members and the broader ecosystem, highlighting their unique journeys, lessons, challenges, and achievements. This week, we are delighted to feature two remarkable leaders: Karmen Čondić-Jurkić , Executive Director & Co-Founder of the Open Molecular Software Foundation, and Savitha Sangameswaran , Senior Research Scientist at Sage Bionetworks. Their stories have moved and inspired us, and we invite you to celebrate glimpses of their journeys with us below. “Success became less about ascending predetermined ladders and more about creating structures that didn't previously exist, and enabling others to do impactful work.” When I look back at my career trajectory, I can clearly see two distinct phases – before and after. The demarcation for these two phases is a profound, yet simple life revelation: I can just do things. While the notion above may be obvious to some, it wasn’t to me. I started my career as an academic scientist with a PhD in computational chemistry, followed by a couple of postdocs. I was taking the well-trodden path for academics, except I started to realize it’s not my path. I wanted to do science, but I didn’t want to be an academic. I wanted better tools for doing science, but I expected someone else to do these things – someone with permission and right credentials, which couldn’t have possibly been me. The only other career option I was aware of was industry, but those roles were not abundant at the time. Entrepreneurship was something done in the US, not really where I was (Croatia and Australia). So you will have to forgive me for taking my time to realize the obvious: I can just do things. This realization catalyzed a fundamental shift in my mindset and launched the “after” phase of my career trajectory. Rather than advancing along predetermined paths, I began pursuing and creating opportunities aligned with emerging interests around open science and research infrastructure, and my values. The early career emphasis on technical mastery and academic pursuit gradually yielded to questions of purpose: What problems truly deserved solving? Which contributions would remain meaningful regardless of recognition? As I allowed these questions to guide decisions, passion naturally followed with a quiet certainty about work worth doing. Success became less about ascending predetermined ladders and more about creating structures that didn't previously exist, and enabling others to do impactful work. This path ultimately led to founding the Open Molecular Software Foundation (OMSF), which owes its existence to a number of people, including myself. That journey for me started from a vague idea I had about seven years ago to a real organization employing more than 20 wonderful people – this achievement is a source of lifelong satisfaction and evidence that enacting change is, in fact, possible. I needed that evidence. So, if you are a reluctant doer like I was, I want to let you know that while permission may never be explicitly granted, the ability to initiate change has been ours all along. — Karmen Čondić-Jurkić , Executive Director & Co-Founder, Open Molecular Software Foundation “I encourage others to seek out mentors, nurture relationships, and surround themselves with people who uplift and inspire them.” Reflecting on my journey toward completing my doctoral degree, I can’t help but recognize how much I’ve grown—both personally and professionally. The road wasn’t easy, especially as I balanced academia, motherhood, and the unpredictable challenges of a global pandemic. But this journey taught me two powerful lessons that I’ll carry with me forever: resilience and self-compassion. There were moments when I felt completely overwhelmed. Between research deadlines, caring for two young children, and navigating global uncertainty, it often seemed like too much to bear. But I realized that resilience isn’t just about pushing through—it’s about embracing the tough moments, reaching out for support, and finding strength in vulnerability. The small steps forward, no matter how slow, still counted as progress. Equally important was learning self-compassion. There were times I felt like I wasn’t doing enough—when I compared myself to others or felt guilty for taking a break. The turning point came when I recognized that pausing didn’t mean failing; it meant recharging so I could continue to show up as my best self. Resilience isn’t about enduring hardships alone. It’s about building a network of support, asking for help when needed, and treating yourself with the same kindness you would offer others. But support systems don’t just appear—they must be cultivated. I encourage others to seek out mentors, nurture relationships, and surround themselves with people who uplift and inspire them. As I move forward, these lessons continue to guide me and I hope they inspire others to seek and build the connections that will help them thrive. — Savitha Sangameswaran , Senior Research Scientist, Sage Bionetworks

title: Bits In Bio Weekly - March 18th 2025
text: 📢 Highlights Google Maps for Cells - HuBMAP Unveils Comprehensive 3D Human Atlas AI Bets Big: Hologen’s $430M Investment Shakes Up Biotech Funding Norms AstraZeneca Bets $1B on EsoBiotec’s ‘Off-the-Shelf’ CAR-T Breakthrough Not yet a member of our super awesome slack community of >8500? Join HERE 🤗 👀 In Case You Missed it .. The 3D Human Reference Atlas Has Been Released by The HuBMAP Consortium The Human BioMolecular Atlas Program (HuBMAP), a consortium of dozens of American research institutions, has created a comprehensive 3D Human Reference Atlas (HRA) of the healthy adult human body, mapping structures from organs down to cells and biomarkers. The HRA v2.0 includes 4,499 anatomical structures, 1,195 cell types, and 2,089 biomarkers from 33 ASCT+B tables and 65 3D reference objects linked to ontologies. New experimental data can be mapped into the HRA using cell type annotation tools, validated antibody panels, or spatial tissue registration. The program provides a flexible hybrid cloud infrastructure with various user interfaces, workflows, and APIs to support atlas construction and usage across multiple platforms. The HRA enables researchers to better understand changes that occur in the human body with aging, disease, and other perturbations, potentially benefiting applications like drug development through improved understanding of disease conditions. The paper also dives into the technical architecture, detailing design decisions regarding the cloud infrastructure provisioned to serve HuBMAP to the public. All HuBMAP data is available online , with details regarding code availability contained in their publication. Behind the Deal: Hologen AI’s $430M Investment in MeiraGTx Breaks the Mold Hologen AI’s $430 million investment in MeiraGTx marks a pivotal moment in biotech funding. Traditionally, AI companies partner with Big Pharma, but Hologen has chosen to directly fund MeiraGTx’s Parkinson’s disease gene therapy, AAV-GAD, from late-stage trials to commercialization. This deal includes a joint venture, Hologen Neuro AI, where Hologen acquires partial ownership of MeiraGTx’s manufacturing subsidiary. It comes with a $200 million upfront payment and an additional $230 million committed capital. AAV-GAD’s promising Phase 2 data showed significant motor function improvements for Parkinson’s patients. Hologen’s AI models were crucial in de-risking future trials by detecting pivotal brain changes. While big pharma companies like Sanofi have previously shown interest , Hologen’s direct investment suggests that AI firms may look to bypass traditional pharma intermediaries. This collaboration underscores AI’s rising strategic value as a driver in drug development. Hologen’s move signals a paradigm shift where AI evolves from a supportive tool to a principal driver, potentially reshaping industry norms. This deal could set a precedent for AI companies to invest in biotech innovation directly, transforming the drug development landscape. J&J AI-assisted lung biopsy software received 510(k) clearance from the FDA. Late-stage lung cancer has one of the lowest 5-year survival rates of just 9%, while early diagnosis significantly improves survival to 64%. However, with only 27.4% of cases detected early, there remains a significant gap in early diagnosis technologies. Johnson & Johnson’s MONARCH™ system hopes to address this isses. Powered by NVIDIA’s computing platform and GE Healthcare’s 3D mobile imaging, it enhances bronchoscopy procedures by guiding physicians through the airways to biopsy hard-to-reach lung nodules using computer vision with real-time 3D imaging to improve targeting accuracy. Last week, the MONARCH™ platform received 510(k) premarket clearance , confirming its equivalence to a legally marketed device and granting approval for U.S. commercialization. AI-assisted medical technology is now a reality, enhancing lung cancer detection and enabling earlier diagnosis for better patient outcomes. For the regulatory nerds out there, t he actual 510(k) is here . EsoBiotech Scores $1 B Deal With AstraZeneca’s For Lentiviral Platform Pharma giant AstraZeneca (AZ) is moving on cell therapy with a $1 billion bet on Belgium’s EsoBiotec, a biotech pioneering in vivo CAR-T technolog y. The dea will see AZ pay $425 million upfront and up to $575 million in milestones, with. EsoBiotec’s proprietary platform, dubbed ENaBL, uses specially engineered lentiviral vectors to deliver genetic instructions to specific immune cells in the patient, “reprogramming” them to attack cancerous or autoreactive cells. In contrast to conventional CAR-T therapy that requires extracting a patient’s T cells, modifying them in a lab, and reinfusing them after toxic lymphodepleting chemotherapy, ENaBL promises an off-the-shelf intravenous treatment with no need for bone marrow preconditioning. The lentiviral vectors are designed to be “immune shielded” – achieved by removing certain (MHC class I antigens and adding CD47 “don’t-eat-me” signals – to prevent the patient’s immune system from clearing them out. This means the virus can persist in vivo and convert resting T cells into tumor-fighting CAR-T cells entirely inside the body, potentially matching the potency of traditional CAR-Ts without the manufacturing lag. For AstraZeneca, the acquisition marks a major acceleration of its cell and gene therapy ambitions. The company has trailed rivals like Novartis, Gilead, and BMS in CAR-T development, so it’s been on a deal-making spree to catch up. RegCell Raises $45.8M and Relocates to US to Tackle Autoimmune Diseases Japan-founded T-cell reprogramming startup, RegCell, Inc., has secured $45.8 million in funding and transitioned its headquarters to the U.S. The financing includes an $8.5 million oversubscribed seed round led by University of Tokyo Edge Capital and $37.3 million in grants from the Japan Agency for Medical Research and Development (AMED). The capital will advance RegCell’s potent epigenetic Treg reprogramming platform into first-in-human trials in 2025, targeting an antigen-specific autoimmune indication. RegCell’s approach leverages the natural biology of regulatory T cells, and was founded by Dr. Shimon Sakaguchi, a pioneering immunologist who originally discovered this cell type. The startup’s move to Emeryville, California positions it to tap into U.S. talent, partnerships, and regulatory expertise, while it continues R&D collaborations in Japan. Sutro Biopharma Cuts CEO, Staff, and Clinical ADC to Conserve Cash Facing substantial financial pressures, Sutro Biopharma announced the departure of CEO Bill Newell, a workforce reduction of nearly 50%, and the termination of its lead clinical-stage antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) , luveltamab tazevibulin, aimed at ovarian cancer. This restructuring move is projected to extend the company’s financial runway by approximately two years, allowing refocusing on early-stage pipeline assets and strategic partnerships. Despite promising Phase 1 data, Sutro determined the significant cost of advancing luveltamab through late-stage trials was unsustainable without immediate partnering opportunities. Sutro now seeks new leadership to guide its pivot towards earlier-stage discovery and development programs, emphasizing cost control and external collaborations. Sutro has raised hundreds of millions of dollars, with this recent move causing a route of remaining investor confidence - at less than a dollar per share, it is now officially a penny stock. Insilico Medicine Secures $110M, Scales Up Robotic-Enhanced Discovery The controversial Bio AI pioneer, Insilico Medicine, has raised $110 million in a Series E funding round, positioning it for a much anticipated expansion in AI-powered drug discovery via robotic laboratory automation. This investment is being led by a new investor Relyens Innovation Santé, and boosts Insilico’s total funding to over $500 million. The company will use the funds to advance clinical trials for its AI-discovered pipeline, including ISM5411 for inflammatory bowel disease and ISM3312, a TEAD inhibitor for cancer. Additionally, Insilico is scaling up its "AI Robotics" division, which it claims automates experimental workflows, significantly accelerating the discovery-to-clinic timeline. The company aims to initiate at least 4 new clinical trials by 2026. Vincerx’s Curious AI Merger Evades A Cash Crisis Bay Area based public company Vincerx Pharma’s fight for survival has taken an unconventional turn into AI. Late last year, the oncology biotech inked a reverse merger with Oqory – a private antibody-drug conjugate developer with a Phase 3 TROP2-targeted therapy – that would have handed 95% of the combined company to Oqory’s owners. But that deal collapsed in early 2025, leaving Vincerx stranded with only $3.9 million in cash (as of Feb 26, 2025) and a runway poised to run dry by late Q2. Now, in a bid to stave off insolvency, Vincerx has signed a letter of intent to merge with QumulusAI, a high-performance computing startup, effectively pivoting from biotech to AI. Under the proposed all-stock deal, QumulusAI’s shareholders would own ~95% of the combined entity (valuing Qumulus at $285 million and Vincerx at a mere $15 million). QumulusAI may also inject a modest $1.5 million into Vincerx’s coffers before closing – a critical lifeline for the cash-strapped firm. The gambit highlights a broader trend: as biotech funding tightens, distressed biotechs are increasingly seeking refuge in the AI boom. SynLlama, A Fine-Tuned Llama 3 Foundation Model, For Synthetic Pathway Generation A UC Berkeley based team has created SynLlama , a fine-tuned version of Meta's open source Llama 3 model that generates both target molecules and their synthetic analogs along with complete synthesis pathways using commercially available building blocks and validated reaction templates. SynLlama delivers comparable or superior performance in synthesis planning, creating synthesizable versions of AI-generated drug candidates, and local hit expansion with improved binding properties. In their paper, the author's highlight its value in real-world applications, including case studies with SARS-CoV-2 main protease inhibitors where it maintained biological activity while improving synthesizability. Notably, SynLlama proposes realistic synthetic pathways that medicinal chemists can readily implement in the laboratory, effectively bridging the critical gap between theoretical AI-driven drug design and practical chemical synthesis. Data and code can be found on GitHub . Maxion Therapeutics Raises $72M to Advance KnotBody Platform UK-based Maxion Therapeutics secured $72 million in an oversubscribed Series A funding round to accelerate its KnotBody antibody platform, which generates small, stable, and selective therapeutic proteins. Led by Medicxi and supported by investors such as Sofinnova Partners and RA Capital, the funding will advance Maxion’s pipeline, including its lead program targeting IL-17A and IL-17F for autoimmune diseases. KnotBodies, derived from cystine-knot miniproteins, offer advantages over traditional antibodies in stability, potency, and manufacturing efficiency. Maxion aims to initiate clinical trials by late 2026, initially focusing on inflammatory and autoimmune conditions with significant unmet medical needs. Arbor Snags $74M to Push In Vivo Gene Editing for Rare Disease Arbor Biotechnologies has secured a $74 million series C to advance its lead CRISPR-based therapy into human trials. The financing, led by Arch Venture Partners and TCGX, extends Arbor’s cash runway into 2027 and will primarily support ABO-101, an in vivo gene-editing treatment for the rare liver disorder primary hyperoxaluria type 1 (PH1). ABO-101 uses a lipid nanoparticle to deliver a Cas12i2 nuclease that knocks out the HAO1 gene in the liver, aiming for a one-time cure by reducing toxic oxalate production. The FDA has granted orphan drug and rare pediatric disease designations to ABO-101, highlighting its therapeutic promise. Arbor’s raise comes amid industry headwinds – many gene editing startups are cutting programs or being acquired – making this influx of capital notable. With Phase 1/2 trials set to begin, Arbor is betting that its novel gene editor and AI-guided enzyme discovery platform can succeed where others have struggled, potentially delivering a breakthrough for PH1 patients. Tempus Expands Oncology Tech with Acquisition of deep6.ai's Clinical Trial Matching Platform Biomedical data giant, Tempus, has acquired a specialized AI-powered clinical trial matching platform deep6.ai , strengthening its precision oncology real world data. This acquisition integrates advanced deep-learning algorithms into Tempus' existing multimodal dataset—covering genomic, transcriptomic, and clinical information from millions of patients—to accurately match oncology patients with suitable clinical trials. The platform’s proprietary technology has demonstrated the capability to accelerate clinical trial enrollment rates by up to 30%, addressing a major bottleneck in oncology research. The terms of the deal have not been disclosed. MedAgentBench Dataset for Clinical LLM Evaluations Stanford researchers have introduced MedAgentBench , a benchmarking dataset designed to evaluate the capabilities of large language model (LLM) agents in clinical settings. Agentic AI enhances LLMs by integrating diverse data sources and tools, enabling automation of administrative tasks such as generating personalized treatment plans, retrieving clinical knowledge, reviewing patient history, updating notes, and managing orders for medications and referrals. This technology has the potential to reduce administrative workload by 70%, allowing clinicians to focus more on patient care.MedAgentBench consists of 300 clinically relevant tasks spanning 100 patients and over 70,000 records. Researchers established baseline performance using state-of-the-art LLMs within a simple "agent orchestrator" framework, where the AI agent can retrieve, update, from various EHR subsystems, or finish the conversation. Among the benchmarks, agents built with Claude 3.5 Sonnet v2 achieved the highest success rate of 69.67%. This highlights the need for further improvements, either by a most sophisticated agent framework to develop reliable AI agents capable of supporting physicians effectively. 🗓️ Upcoming BiB Events See our handy dandy Lu.ma event calendar HERE , please RSVP so folks can plan accordingly!
