title: Bits In Bio Weekly - March 11th 2025
text: 📢 Highlights Flagship Pioneering Bets $200M for Autonomous Scientific AI Play - Lila Sciences Daiichi Sankyo Teams with Nosis Bio for AI-Powered RNA Delivery BMS Buys 2seventy Bio for $286M, Gains Full Control of CAR-T Abecma Not yet a member of our super awesome slack community of ˜9000? Join HERE 🤗 👀 In Case You Missed it .. Lila Sciences Launched With $200M From Flagship Pioneering to Develop Scientific Superintelligence Flagship Pioneering has unveiled Lila Sciences , an AI-driven biotech startup focused on developing a scientific superintelligence that will one day autonomously conduct research and accelerate discovery with minimal humans in the loop. With an initial $200 million seed investment, the company is designing AI models capable of hypothesis generation, experiment execution, and data-driven scientific reasoning. Lila Sciences will integrate its AI platform with interal automated laboratories to enable real-time iteration across life sciences, chemistry, and high-margin materials science. This initiative is a part of Flagships deepening commitment to pursuing AI-driven scientific discovery, in line with the broader recent surge of AI-based lab automation startups and computational-first biotech models. If successful, Lila Sciences' audacious bet could fundamentally alter how foundational scientific knowledge is created, shifting from human-led experimentation to machine-driven innovation at scale. Time will tell how different Lila is compared to Boston-born scientific superintelligence startup Xaira , launched by Flagship contemporaries Arch Venture Partners and Foresight labs with $1 Billion "seed” round of funding. Behind the Deal: Daiichi Sankyo taps Nosis Bio’s AI to unlock RNA therapies Daiichi Sankyo made a significant move into AI-driven drug delivery by teaming up with Berkely-based Nosis Bio . The new partnership with undisclosed terms leverages Nosis’s Connexa AI platform to develop precise RNA therapies aimed at tissues traditionally seen as tough targets. Nosis’s Connexa platform uses sophisticated AI models to design targeted RNA delivery at the atomic level, addressing a core challenge: delivering RNA treatments safely and effectively beyond the liver. This advancement could dramatically broaden the therapeutic possibilities of RNA-based treatments. Daiichi aims to develop treatments for conditions affecting the heart, lungs, kidneys, muscles, and brain.Strategically, the deal fits perfectly into pharma’s broader push toward innovation, driven by investor expectations. Companies like Daiichi are under increasing pressure to demonstrate they're embracing cutting-edge technologies to maintain competitiveness. Importantly, J&J also recently partnered with Nosis Bio for similar goals , highlighting broader industry confidence in Connexa’s potential. Full Control of Abecma Secured by Bristol Myers Squibb's $286M Acquisition of 2seventy Bio Bristol Myers Squibb has announced its $286 million acquisition of 2seventy bio , gaining full rights to Abecma (idecabtagene vicleucel), a BCMA-targeted CAR-T therapy for relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma. Previously co-developed with Massachusetts based 2seventy bio, Abecma generated $406 million in global sales in 2024, with BMS paying $43 million in profit-sharing to its partner. The deal eliminates future revenue-sharing obligations while reinforcing BMS’s cell therapy portfolio, where it competes with Carvykti (Johnson & Johnson and Legend Biotech’s BCMA CAR-T). The acquisition represents an 88% premium over 2seventy’s last closing price, signaling BMS’s commitment to expanding its dominance in hematologic malignancies. The transaction is expected to close by Q2 2025, pending regulatory approval. Deep Learning Model Predicts Lung Cancer Mutations from Histology Images with 96% Precision, San Genomic Sequencing While accurate identification of driver gene mutations is essential for guiding treatment and prognosis in lung cancer, genetic sequencing remains inaccessibly costly to many. Researchers at Guangzhou Medical University developed a new diagnostic deep learning mode l to predict the mutation status of four key driver genes using routinely acquired histological images rather than expense next generation sequencing e.g. Illumina. External validation on independent datasets, including TCGA, demonstrated high accuracy for EGFR mutations (96% precision, 95% recall), which are critical for selecting targeted therapies like tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs). The model achieved moderate performance for three additional biomarkers (median 85% precision, 83% recall). These results highlight AI-powered histological analysis as a scalable, cost-effective alternative to genomic sequencing for lung cancer mutation detection. Further clinical validation is required to assess the benefits of AI-predicted gene mutations in patient care, but this method nonetheless provides a timely sense of hope as resource constraints come into view, driven by shifting geopolitical and economic forces. Jazz Pharmaceuticals to Acquire Chimerix for $935M, Expanding Rare Oncology Portfolio Chimerix has sealed an acquisition offer from Jazz Pharmaceuticals for $935 million , strengthening its pipeline in rare and aggressive cancers. The acquisition is centered around dordaviprone (ONC201), a first-in-class dopamine receptor D2 (DRD2) antagonist and ClpP activator being developed for H3 K27M-mutant diffuse midline glioma (DMG)—a fatal pediatric brain tumor with no approved treatments. Currently under FDA review, dordaviprone has demonstrated promising overall survival benefits in clinical studies, with a decision expected by August 18, 2025. Jazz will pay $170 million upfront, with additional milestone-based payouts, aligning with its strategy of expanding in precision oncology and orphan indications. The deal follows Jazz’s recent acquisitions, including GW Pharmaceuticals for cannabinoid-based epilepsy treatments, showcasing the company’s aggressive expansion in neuroscience and oncology. 🗓️ Upcoming BiB Events See our handy dandy Lu.ma event calendar HERE , please RSVP so folks can plan accordingly! title: Bits In Bio Weekly - March 4th 2025
text: 📢 Highlights Arc Institute and Vevo Therapeutics Open Source  Tahoe-100M, World’s Largest Single-Cell Dataset Sofinnova Closes Out $1.2B+ for High-Stakes Biotech Innovation, Securing 2024 Capital Commitments Recent $885M Genesis Therapeutics Deal Highlights Merck’s Burgeoning AI Ambitions Not yet a member of our super awesome slack community of >8500? Join HERE 🤗 👀 In Case You Missed it .. Arc Institute Release Largest Single-Cell Drug Response Dataset with Vevo Vevo Therapeutics, in collaboration with the Arc Institute, has unveiled the Tahoe-100M, the world's largest open-source single-cell drug response atlas . This dataset comprises over 100 million single-cell transcriptomic profiles, detailing cellular responses to 1,200 small-molecule drug perturbations across 50 cancer cell lines. Developed using Vevo's Mosaic platform, the dataset captures cellular responses across 60,000 experimental conditions, offering  insights into gene function, signaling pathways, and potential feedback mechanisms. The Tahoe-100M is now accessible via the Arc Virtual Cell Atlas, a public resource integrating single-cell transcriptomic data from over 300 million cells across various species, tissues, and experimental conditions.​ You can also find it on their github . Sofinnova Partners Raises Over $1 Billion for New Biotech Investments One of Europes’ leading life science venture capital groups ,53 year old Sofinnova Partners, has announced it has successfully closed the over $1.2 billion of funds committed late 2024 despite challenges. This significant capital infusion will be directed towards substantial investment into as many of 50 early-stage therapeutics, medical devices, and digital health startups, emphasizing high-risk, high-reward biotech innovations. With over 500 companies in its portfolio, Sofinnova's track record includes backing transformative biotech firms, with over 40 IPOs and notable M&A exits, such as Corvidia's $2.1 billion acquisition by Novo Nordisk. This substantial fundraise reflects sustained EU investor confidence in the biotech sector, despite broader venture capital market challenges.​ Behind the Deal: Incyte’s Bold $885M Bet on Genesis AI Showcases Pharma’s Drive for AI Partnerships In a defining move for biotech’s embrace of artificial intelligence, Incyte has forged an $885 million partnership with Genesis Therapeutics . This collaboration aims to leverage Genesis’s GEMS AI platform to develop small-molecule medicines targeting undisclosed diseases. The financial structure includes a $30 million upfront payment, with substantial milestones making up the balance, underscoring this AI-driven approach's high risks and potential. This Incyte-Genesis partnership reflects a broader industry shift towards integrating AI in drug discovery, seen as pivotal in accelerating the bench-to-bedside development timeline. Similarly, the announcement of a near $700 million merger between Recursion Pharmaceuticals and Exscientia in Aug 2024 underscores the scale of investment and expectation in AI capabilities. This merger, described by Recursion’s CFO as analogous to the biologics revolution , highlights the transformative potential of AI in pharma. Additionally, the recent merger between Renovaro Biosciences and BioSymetrics focuses on harnessing AI for biomarker discovery and precision medicine, particularly in oncology. These AI-driven initiatives promise to enhance drug pipelines and increase shareholder value by demonstrating a commitment to cutting-edge technology and potential market leadership in personalized medicine. This strategic focus is crucial in attracting investments and maintaining competitive advantages in a rapidly evolving pharmaceutical landscape. Lab-in-the-loop therapeutic antibody design with deep learning A new preprint released by a team at Genentech discusses their specific take on the ideal "Lab-in-the-loop" strategy for designing antibodies. The team focused on optimizing binding affinity and expression against four clinically relevant antigen targets: EGFR, IL-6, HER2, and OSM. Their strategy utilized various machine learning methods to generate improved variants of lead antibody candidates obtained from animal immunization. The paper details how they used various generative models (including variational diffusion models and Bayesian optimization approaches), multi-task property prediction, active learning for design selection, and efficient experimental workflows. The team ultimately performed four iterative rounds of optimization and produced "3–100× higher binding affinity antibodies for ten lead molecules to four different antigen targets." The authors frame the “Lab-in-the-loop” strategy as a paradigm shift necessary for effective navigation of the complex multi-property optimization problem that therapeutic antibody design represents. Check out the code here . Eikon Therapeutics Secures $350 Million Series D to Advance Oncology Pipeline The Bay-area biotech , Eikon Therapeutics, has raised a $350 million in a Series D funding round to advance its clinical-stage oncology pipeline. The financing, led by existing and new investors including The Column Group and ARCH Venture Partners, will accelerate the company's small-molecule programs targeting hard-to-treat cancers. Eikon's proprietary real-time imaging technology enables precise measurement of intracellular protein dynamics, offering novel insights into drug-target interactions. With multiple trials underway, the company is positioning itself as a leader in precision oncology, which it will need to be in order to satisfy the over 1.1 billion it has raised to date. 23andMe's Market Valuation Plummets as CEO’s 2nd Take-Private Bid is Rejected Once valued at $5.8 billion, the once-mighty 23&me now sees its market capitalization well below $40 million, serving as a potent referendum on its controversial CEO Anne Wojcicki and a complete lack of investor confidence in the enterprise. Yesterday, the company's board rejected a second take-private bid from its CEO , valuing the company at $40M. The decline follows multiple rounds of layoffs, consumer disappointment, ethics probes, and the company's failure to pivot successfully into either diagnostic or drug development businesses. A recent hacking incident exposed personal details of 7 million customers, leading to legal and financial issues. Co-founder Anne Wojcicki had first proposed taking the company private in 2024, offering to buy shares at a fraction of their IPO price, which led to the resignation of the entire board which included the head of Sequoia Capital and the CEO of Youtube.. The company is shifting focus from consumer genetic testing to marketing its genetic data to pharmaceutical firms, abandoning its cost-intensive drug development programs. There is growing concern about the company's ability to monetize its genetic database without damaging the privacy of the customers who once readily trusted them. New Deep Learning Model Enhances Immunotherapy Patient Selection A team at AstraZeneca has introduced the Clinical Transformer, an explainable AI system that can identify patients likely to benefit from immunotherapy, even with limited clinical data. The model outperformed existing methods across 12 cancer datasets comprising over 140,000 patients by using advanced machine learning techniques. The system's generative capability enables in silico perturbation experiments to test hypothetical treatment scenarios. By analyzing immune-associated features, researchers identified patient subsets that may benefit from immunotherapy. The paper highlights the potential of using the internal representations generated by the deep learning model for specialized applications such as predicting immunotherapy responses in limited datasets from real-world settings or early clinical trials. Through its explainability and perturbation modules, the Clinical Transformer can also potentially enhance clinical interpretability by pinpointing the specific molecular and clinical factors that drive individual patient outcomes and treatment responses. The code can be found here . Novo Nordisk Offers $354M For Tissue Specific Targeting from Gensaic Cambridge-based Gensaic, Inc. has announced a license and discovery collaboration with Novo Nordisk to develop tissue-targeting ligands for precision medicine. The partnership leverages Gensaic's proprietary FORGE™ engine, which combines AI-powered protein design with biological insights to discover ligands for tissue-selective intracellular delivery. The collaboration aims to discover tissue-targeting ligands and develop new therapeutic candidates to treat cardiometabolic diseases via undisclosed targets. Under the agreement, Gensaic is eligible to receive up to $354 million in upfront payments, development, and commercial milestones per target, plus tiered royalties. Novo Nordisk will also reimburse research and development costs and participate in a future financing round.​ 🗓️ Upcoming BiB Events See our handy dandy Lu.ma event calendar HERE , please RSVP so folks can plan accordingly! title: Bits In Bio Weekly - February 25th 2025
text: 📢 Highlights Evo 2 Sets a New Benchmark for AI in Biology—Predicting Mutations at Unprecedented Scale DeepMind Releases AI ‘Co-Scientist’ to Accelerate Hypothesis Generation Salt Lake City’s Altitude Lab Steps Up to Support Startups Amidst Federal Funding  Uncertainty Not yet a member of our super awesome slack community of >8500? Join HERE 🤗 👀 In Case You Missed it .. Evo 2, the Largest AI Model in Biology to Date, Predicst Genetic Mutation Effects A multidisciplinary collaboration between Arc Institute, University of California Berkeley, NVIDIA, and more have introduced Evo 2, an AI model designed to predict harmful genetic mutations and generate small eukaryotic genomes. Building on the capabilities of Evo 1, which focused on bacterial genomes, Evo 2 expands its scope to genetic sequences across various life forms, including microbes, plants, and humans. The model is trained on 9.3 trillion nucleotides from 128,000 species and has 40 billion parameters, enabling broader predictive capabilities than earlier biological AI models. It can efficiently detect pathogenic mutations and generate DNA sequences comparable in scale to yeast chromosomes or small bacterial genomes. Researchers can access Evo 2 through an API and further refine it. You can find the code, courtesy of the Arc Institute, at their Github repo . Accelerating Scientific Breakthroughs with an AI ‘Co-Scientist’ Google DeepMind has unveiled an AI-powered “co-scientist” built on its Gemini 2.0 model to act as a virtual collaborator in biomedical research. This multi-agent system can autonomously generate hypotheses, analyze data, and propose experiments , digesting vast scientific literature far faster than a human could. Their approach allegedly goes beyond surface-level analysis, using advanced models to propose hypotheses and even design new experiments. Importantly, it’s designed to augment rather than replace human scientists: early trials (e.g. in liver fibrosis) showed the AI suggesting promising solutions and even rediscovering known mechanisms, all while remaining a collaborative tool that amplifies scientists’ productivity​Early trials demonstrated significant reductions in the time it takes to validate research findings, hinting that labs might accelerate hypothesis-generation stages of scientific discovery in select domains. At 70 pages, their research paper is not for the faint of heart. Safety Net from Altitude in Salt Lake City Amid NIH Grant Concerns Worried about shaky NIH funding, Altitude Lab (a Salt Lake City biotech incubator backed by Recursion CEO Chris Gibson) is stepping in to support early-stage biotechs with alternative funding and lab space outside the traditional grant system .This move comes as many young companies worry about reduced NIH budgets, which traditionally fuel early-stage research through grants. Altitude’s model reduces overhead for startups, letting them focus on validating new therapeutic modalities without the financial strain of maintaining standalone facilities. Meanwhile, founders and scientists can share best practices and resources, nurturing a mini-ecosystem of collaboration. The fund will offer: $100,000–$250,000 in pre-seed investment capital, 12 months of lab and office space at Altitude Lab, Access to Altitude Lab’s accelerator program, including mentorship from industry leaders and connections to national investors. The hope is that by pooling risks and resources, the sector remains resilient—even if government funding becomes less predictable. Interested parties can submit an applications here . Agentic AI in Healthcare form NVIDIA and IQVIA Alliance NVIDIA and IQVIA have struck a partnership to develop “ agentic AI ” models tailor-made for healthcare and life sciences, essentially turning AI systems into active research agents rather than passive tools. By leveraging NVIDIA’s GPU clusters, IQVIA hopes to run complex models in record time, speeding up clinical trial simulations and real-world evidence studies. They’re also exploring federated learning techniques, which allow algorithms to train on decentralized data without compromising patient privacy. This architecture could unlock new insights in oncology, rare diseases, and beyond by accelerating the search for treatment patterns hidden in huge datasets. Incyte’s $885M AI Pact with Genesis For Drug Discovery Biopharma giant Incyte is entering a strategic collaboration worth up to $900 million with Genesis Gems , a startup using AI to pinpoint promising drug targets and molecules. Under the deal, Genesis Gems’ proprietary “GEMS” platform will analyze vast compound libraries to predict which structures have the highest therapeutic potential. Incyte aims to accelerate its pipeline, reducing early-stage guesswork by leveraging machine-driven insights. By coupling Genesis’s AI firepower with Incyte’s medicinal chemistry know-how, the partners aim to discover new drug candidates faster than traditional chemistry methods, potentially tackling diseases that have stumped conventional approaches​. Ultimately, both parties hope this synergy will yield new treatments faster and more cost-effectively than traditional approaches. Achira.ai’s AI-Powered Diagnostics Courtesy of Chan Zuckerberg Initiative A new startup spun out of a Chan Zuckerberg Initiative – Achira.ai - has launched with a $33 million seed round (backed by NVIDIA’s venture arm) to build a hybrid AI + physics platform for biotechAchira.ai specializes in microfluidic platforms that run diagnostics at the point of care—think rapid tests for infectious diseases, hormone levels, and more—enhanced by machine learning insights. By integrating AI algorithms directly on-chip, they can analyze samples in real time without needing full lab infrastructure. The company also focuses on making these tests more affordable and user-friendly, which is a big plus for remote clinics or developing regions. Their solutions aim to minimize manual handling errors and expedite treatment decisions, boosting patient outcomes. AI-Driven Robotics Lab Allegedly Uncovers TNIK Inhibition Effect on Aging In a glimpse of how autonomous labs can speed up science, a n AI-driven robotics laboratory (led by Insilico Medicine researchers) has allegedly discovered that inhibiting TNIK (Traf2- and Nck-interacting kinase) may combat the inflammatory effects of aging . ​Rather than killing senescent cells outright, these so-called “senomorphic” treatments aim to restore their function—potentially improving tissue health and longevity. By automating the search through large compound libraries, researchers discovered how blocking TNIK might reduce inflammation and bolster cellular repair pathways. The technique underscores the promise of integrated AI and robotics to identify new targets far more rapidly than manual methods. If validated clinically, it could expand the portfolio of anti-aging therapies beyond traditional senolytics. Epitopea to Target Hidden Tumor Antigens in Merck Alliance Biopharma giatn Merck & Co. is teaming up with cancer biotech Epitopea to hunt down previously unknown tumor antigens hidden in the genome’s “junk DNA” – aiming to expand the target list for next-gen cancer immunotherapies​ Merck has teamed up with Epitopea to mine so-called “cryptic” tumor antigens—unique molecular markers often hidden from standard detection methods. By focusing on these overlooked targets, the collaboration seeks to develop next-generation immunotherapies that can bypass resistance mechanisms in difficult-to-treat cancers. The companies plan to integrate Epitopea’s novel antigen discovery platform with Merck’s immuno-oncology research pipeline, aiming for highly personalized approaches. This partnership also underscores the industry’s growing interest in deeper tumor profiling, where advanced analytics help reveal stealthier cancer signatures. 🗓️ Upcoming BiB Events See our handy dandy Lu.ma event calendar HERE , please RSVP so folks can plan accordingly! title: Bits In Bio Weekly - February 18th 2025
text: 📢 Highlights David vs. Goliath - OpenProtein.AI Challenges Status Quo with Open-Source Protein Model PoEt-2 Another Elite Bio AI Protein Startup, Latent Labs Bets on Programmable Biology Over Drug Development Biogen Doubles Down on RNA Therapies with $550M Total Stoke Partnership Targeting Dravets Syndrome Not yet a member of our super awesome slack community of >8500? Join HERE 🤗 👀 In Case You Missed it .. OpenProtein.AI Unveils Open-Source Multimodal Protein Model The open source project OpenProtein.AI has introduced PoET-2, a new multimodal foundation model for protein design that aims to bring unprecedented control to protein engineering. Unlike conventional protein AI models that learn from sequences, PoET-2 simultaneously learns from protein sequences and structural data, with the goal of extracting fundamental principles that govern protein function so that researchers can prompt PoET-2 to generate proteins with specific properties – a form of “controllable” protein generation – rather than a one-size-fits-all outputs. PoET-2 achieves state-of-the-art performance in protein modeling despite its relatively small size of 182 million parameters, matching the accuracy of trillion-parameter models by efficiently integrating multiple data modalities. The model is being released as an open-source platform , allowing even the most modest academic and startup labs to access capabilities currently reserved for those with deep pockets. This open approach stands in contrast to the closed, proprietary systems of some tech giants, and industry voices are already noting its significance. With heavy investment flowing into AI-driven protein engineering, OpenProtein’s open-source model could accelerate advances in everything from enzyme design to antibody discovery. Find its github here . Latent Labs Emerges From Stealth with $50M Make Biology “programmable” A London biotechnology startup founded by DeepMind alumnus, Latent Labs, has emerged from stealth mode with $50 million in funding . Like its contemporaries, the company aims to develop AI foundation models that make biology programmable, with an initial focus on designing novel proteins for therapeutic applications. The funding round, co-led by Radical Ventures and Sofinnova Partners, will support the expansion of Latent Labs' team and accelerate the development of its AI-driven platform. Its founder previously co-led the protein design team at DeepMind and set up DeepMind’s wet lab at the Francis Crick Institute in London. Latent Labs states that it won’t be developing its own therapeutics but will rather partner with third-parties and focus on expediting and de-risking the earlier R&D stages. With new protein-focused Bio AI platform companies emerging weekly, the question now who will survive in what is becoming an increasingly competitive space. Behind the Deal: Biogen’s Strategic Partnership with Stoke Therapeutics to Advance Dravet Syndrome Treatment In a strategic move to bolster its neurology portfolio, Biogen has entered into a collaboration with Stoke Therapeutics, investing $165 million upfron t for ex-U.S. rights to zorevunersen, a promising antisense oligonucleotide (ASO) therapy for Dravet syndrome. This agreement includes potential milestone payments up to $385 million, positioning Biogen to commercialize zorevunersen outside the U.S., Canada, and Mexico, while Stoke retains rights within these territories and leads global development efforts. Dravet syndrome, a severe genetic epilepsy, is often linked to mutations in the SCN1A gene. Zorevunersen aims to address this root cause by increasing the expression of the NaV1.1 protein, potentially offering the first disease-modifying therapy for the condition. Clinical studies have shown that zorevunersen not only reduces convulsive seizures but also improves cognition and behavior. Stoke plans to initiate a global Phase 3 trial in the second quarter of 2025, with pivotal data anticipated by late 2027. This partnership is notable for its structure, granting Biogen exclusive commercialization rights in select territories outside of North America while allowing Stoke to maintain control over development and North American markets. This approach enables both companies to leverage their strengths and aligns with Biogen’s strategic focus on RNA-targeted therapies.For Stoke, the deal provides essential funding, extending its financial runway through mid-2028 and facilitating the advancement of Zorevunersen toward potential regulatory approvals. New Tooling Enables Computational Design of Novel Serine Hydrolases David Baker Lab’s paper ‘ Computational design of serine hydrolases ’ was published this week in Science , marking a major step forward in AI-driven enzyme design by leveraging deep learning methodologies to create novel serine hydrolases from scratch. Using RFdiffusion, a state-of-the-art generative AI model, the researchers designed de novo protein scaffolds tailored to house complex catalytic sites with sub-angstrom precision. Furthermore, the development of ChemNet, an ensemble-based neural network, enabled the assessment of active site geometry and preorganization across multiple steps of the catalytic cycle. The designed serine hydrolases adopt novel structural folds absent in nature, demonstrating the power of computational methods to generate entirely new protein architectures that extend beyond evolutionary constraints. Atomwise Struggles as CEO Steve Worland Takes Over To Provide Sability Once a high-flyer in AI-driven drug discovery, Atomwise has appointed Steve Worland, Ph.D. as its new CEO whilst , replacing Abraham Heifets, the founding CEO who led Atomwise since 2012, marking a significant changing of the guard. The leadership shake-up comes amid reports that Atomwise’s momentum has stalled – the company, known for its AtomNet platform, had amassed a multi-billion-dollar deal pipeline with big pharma partners in its early years but recently the flow of new partnerships appears to have slowed. There are signs of financial strain as well: Atomwise reportedly raised a $45 million Series C round (far smaller than its previous financing) as a stopgap. Increasing competition from a new generation of AI drug discovery firms has put added pressure on Atomwise to show tangible progress. While it was a pioneer in using AI to find small-molecule hits, rivals like Insilico and Exscientia have since leapfrogged by advancing AI-designed drugs into clinical trials. With Worland – a pharma veteran credited on multiple drug programs – now at the helm, Atomwise is expected to refocus its pipeline and strategy for more concrete results It remains to be seen whether a fresh direction can help Atomwise regain its footing or if the company will become a cautionary tale of early promise fizzling out in the competitive AI drug discovery arena. Terrain Biosciences Launches from Stealth Mode with $9M Seed Cambridge-based Terrain Biosciences has officially emerged from stealth, positioning itself as a new contender in RNA-based therapeutics and vaccine development, billing itself as the world’s first “RNA design-build” company, combining AI with a proprietary manufacturing platform to create better RNA faster. The goal is to tackle long standing challenges in RNA therapy – from improving the stability and delivery of mRNA vaccines to optimizing RNA constructs for gene and cell therapies. The early signs are promising, with Terrain’s claiming it has helped over a dozen biotech partners design RNA sequences with greater expression and durability than before, but is not naming names. While the company hasn’t publicly detailed its partnerships, it has raised an initial $9 million seed round to fuel its tech development. Insilico Medicine Releases AI Benchmark for Drug Discovery Amidst recent scrutiny of AbSci, Insilico Medicine has published a first-of-its-kind preclinical benchmark dataset showcasing how its AI-designed drug candidates perform in the lab. In a move toward greater transparency, the company detailed development timelines and success rates for 22 small-molecule programs discovered via its AI platform. The data provide a rare window into the efficiency of AI-driven drug discovery: on average, Insilico reached a Preclinical Candidate (PCC) nomination in about 13 months – dramatically faster than the traditional 2.5–4 year timeline. Moreover, of those 22 AI-designed candidates, 10 have already secured FDA IND clearance to proceed to human trial. The benchmark report includes validated preclinical metrics (enzymatic assays, ADME profiles, animal pharmacokinetics and efficacy studies, etc.) for each candidate, allowing outsiders to gauge the robustness of Insilico’s approach. This transparency is significant – investors and researchers can now inspect real-world outcomes, not just AI hype, when evaluating such companies. Insilico’s CEO has framed the release as part of an effort to establish industry-wide standards for AI in drug discovery, addressing the current lack of benchmarking in the field. The timing couldn’t be more interesting: it comes as another AI drug player, Absci, is under the microscope for bold claims about “AI-designed biologics” that critics say haven’t been backed up by much substance. Absci’s CEO had famously bragged about being the first to design new antibodies with “zero-shot” generative AI , supposedly removing the need for any traditional wet-lab discover. But skeptics ( and a recent STAT investigation ) suggest those claims were overblown. New PE-Backed Neuro Biotech Launches with Roche Assets and $93.5 Million A new private equity-backed biotech called Newleos Therapeutics has launched with a $93.5 million financing and a portfolio of neuroscience drugs courtesy of pharma giant Roche Backed by venture firm Longwood Fund, Newleos is focused on advancing 4 total neurodegenerative and neuropsychiatric drug candidates - all of which come from Roche’s central nervous system pipeline, covering conditions in psychiatry and potentially other brain disorders, and were “cast-offs” not because they failed outright, but because Roche re-prioritized its R&D efforts. Not-so-coincidentally, the company’s leadership includes former Roche scientists and executives who helped shepherd these very compounds, which should smooth the transition and perhaps allow for new trial designs or niche indications that a large pharma wouldn’t pursue. Obviously success is not guaranteed – there’s a reason these drugs were nearly discontinued – but if Newleos can unlock value through smarter development or patient selection, it could turn Roche’s leftovers into tomorrow’s neurological therapies. FDA Food Division Chief Resigns Amid Industry and Political Shifts The head of the FDA’s food division has abruptly resigned , underscoring growing turmoil over food safety regulation in the US. Jim Jones, appointed in 2023 as the first Deputy Commissioner for Human Foods, stepped down this week after the administration ordered sweeping and deeply criticized staff layoffs in his department. In a blunt resignation letter, Jones cited the new administration’s “indiscriminate” firing of 89 food safety staff – including key experts on infant formula safety – and said it would be “fruitless” for him to continue given the leadership’s apparent “disdain” for the experts needed to keep food safe. The political backdrop is stark: the new administration in Washington has been pushing aggressive, industry-friendly policies, slashing away opposition. These FDA food unit layoffs appear to be part of that agenda, effectively gutting resources for oversight when it is direly needed - Jones’s departure comes on the heels of several high-profile food contamination crises that had already put FDA under scrutiny – from the 2022 infant formula contamination and shortage , to recurring outbreaks of food-borne illness linked to produce and packaged foods. It also arrives as the agency wrestles with how to regulate emerging food technologies like lab-grown meat and precision-fermented. The FDA’s Human Foods Program was only recently overhauled (partly in response to the formula fiasco) and had begun rolling out stronger nutrition and safety measures – such as banning a long-used carcinogenic dye and proposing new front-of-package labeling for health warnings. Now, with Jones out and a wave of staff cuts, those initiatives and the overall direction of U.S. food policy are in flux. In short, the balance of power in food regulation is shifting – and everything from what goes on nutrition labels to how rigorously lab-grown foods are vetted may be reshaped in the coming months. 🗓️ Upcoming BiB Events See our handy dandy Lu.ma event calendar HERE , please RSVP so folks can plan accordingly! title: Bits In Bio Weekly - February 11th 2025
text: 📢 Highlights ML vs. Physics-Based Drug Discovery? New AI from Oxford and Boehringer Goes After Free Energy Perturbation (FEP) Molecular Modeling Recursion’s AI-Repurposed Drug Shows Phase 1 Promise for Rare Brain Disease Novartis’ $925M Cardiovascular Power Play - Reacquisition of Former Spinout Anthos Not yet a member of our super awesome slack community of >8500? Join HERE 🤗 👀 In Case You Missed it .. Narrowing The Gap Between ML Scoring Functions and Free Energy Perturbation with Augmented Data and GNNs Accurate binding affinity prediction is a key challenge in computational drug discovery, particularly for optimizing lead compounds. This paper , the result of a collaboration between The University of Oxford and Boehringer Ingelheim, introduces AEV-PLIG , an attention-based graph neural network (GNN) that models protein-ligand interactions by combining atomic environment vectors (AEVs) and protein-ligand interaction graphs (PLIGs). The study also presents an out-of-distribution (OOD) benchmark to improve generalizability, addressing existing models' common limitations. Additionally, integrating augmented data from molecular docking and template-based modeling enhances predictive performance. AEV-PLIG demonstrates competitive results, with the model’s prediction accuracy approaching that of Free Energy Perturbation (FEP+) for congeneric series of ligands binding to the same protein while offering significant computational efficiency. These findings highlight the potential of advanced machine learning techniques in accelerating drug discovery workflows. The team made their code available on Github, which you can find here . Recursion’s AI-Discovered Brain Drug’s Promising Phase 1 Results With the target of cerebral cavernous malformation (CCM)​ in its crosshairs, Recursion Pharmaceuticals has reported encouraging Phase 1 results for REC-994, a small-molecule therapy discovered via its machine-learning platform to treat the rare neurovascular condition. In the trial, patients on oral REC-994 exhibited a strong safety profile and even showed reductions in the volume of cerebral and brainstem lesions caused by CCM​. This marks one of only a few real-world successes for an AI-designed drug in human trials – a significant milestone that lends credibility to the wide-spread albeit contentious belief that applied artificial intelligence is making a positive impact on drug discovery​. Buoyed by these data, Recursion is preparing to advance REC-994 into larger clinical studies and continues to apply its “AI-first” pipeline to other rare and intractable diseases. If REC-994 ultimately proves effective, it could set a precedent for accelerating drug discovery timelines through AI, offering hope that machine learning can speed up the development of new biopharma treatments. Behind the Deal: Novartis Reacquires Anthos in Strategic Cardiovascular Play Novartis' $925 million acquisition of Anthos Therapeutics brings abelacimab, a promising blood clot-busting candidate, full circle. This deal, potentially reaching $3.1 billion with milestones, showcases an intriguing pharmaceutical strategy. In 2019, Novartis spun out abelacimab to form Anthos, partnering with Blackstone Life Sciences who invested $250 million . This move externalized development risks while Novartis retained a minority stake. Abelacimab's journey has been marked by significant achievements. It received two FDA Fast Track Designations in 2022 : for cancer-associated thrombosis and stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation. A Phase 2 trial was halted early due to "overwhelming reduction in bleeding" compared to standard care. The timing of this reacquisition is strategic. With abelacimab now in Phase 3 trials and showing promise, Novartis can leverage its cardiovascular expertise to potentially bring a first-in-class treatment to market. This deal also reflects the competitive anticoagulant landscape. While some factor XI inhibitors have faced setbacks, abelacimab's strong performance positions Novartis favorably in this evolving field. The acquisition exemplifies a full-cycle approach in drug development, from initial research to spinout and eventual reacquisition, demonstrating Novartis' adaptable strategy in bringing innovative treatments to patients. OliX Strike $630M Deal with Eli Lilly for siRNA-Based MASH (NASH) Therapy Pharma giant Eli Lilly is betting big on RNA interference with a deal worth up to $630 million to license OliX Pharmaceuticals’ early-stage treatment for metabolic dysfunction associated steatohepatitis (MASH, formerly NASH)​. The candidate, OLX-702A, is a small interfering RNA (siRNA) therapeutic designed to silence the MARC1 gene – a liver enzyme target implicated in fibrosis and metabolic disease progression​. MASH has frustrated drug developers for years, with over 20 clinical candidates failing due to the disease’s complex biology and a lack of reliable biomarkers to track efficacy​. By using an RNAi approach to directly knock down a fibrosis-driving gene in the liver, Lilly hopes to succeed where traditional small molecules have struggled, potentially reducing liver scarring and improving metabolic outcomes as suggested by preclinical models​. A successful program in this area could spotlight RNA-based therapies as game-changers in metabolic liver disease – an arena that until now has been dominated by conventional small molecules and biologics. Receptor.AI and Moexa Join Forces for AI-Designed SMAD3 Inhibitors AI-driven biotech startup Receptor.AI is partnering with Moexa Pharmaceuticals to develop novel small-molecule inhibitors of SMAD3 for fibrotic and inflammatory diseases (with potential applications in oncology as well)​. SMAD3 is a key mediator of the TGF-β signaling pathway, which makes it a central driver of pathological fibrosis, immune system dysregulation, and certain cancer progressions. The collaboration will deploy generative AI models to accelerate the hit-to-lead process – ideally resulting in a swiftly designed suite of high-affinity SMAD3 inhibitors in a fraction of the time it would take using traditional methods​. Moexa will bring its wet-lab expertise by synthesizing the AI-predicted molecules and evaluating them in preclinical assays (it already has some early SMAD3 inhibitor s showing promising effects in animal models)​, effectively bridging Receptor.AI’s in silico predictions with real-world experimental validation. This alliance, should it prove successful, could produce first-in-class therapies targeting SMAD3, addressing unmet needs in fibrosis-driven conditions that currently lack effective targeted treatments. Prostate Cancer Combo From Pfizer Cuts Progression by 49% in Phase 1 Following an accidental data leak, Pfizer (fully) unveiled first-in-human data showing that its investigational CD47 inhibitor (maplirpacept) combined with enzalutamide achieved a 49% reduction in the risk of disease progression or death for patients with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC). CD47 is known as the "don't eat me" signal that cancer cells use to evade destruction by macrophages (immune cells), so blocking this pathway aims to unmask tumor cells to the immune system​. While therapies targeting CD47 have mostly been tested in blood cancers, these results hint that the approach can translate to solid tumors like prostate cancer, opening a new frontier in immuno-oncology​. Given that many prostate tumors eventually develop resistance to hormone blockers like enzalutamide, adding a CD47 blockade could help the immune system clear out residual cancer cells that standard therapy leaves behind. Pfizer is now gearing up for the next trial phases, looking to confirm the benefit in a larger patient population and to explore how CD47 inhibition can synergize with existing prostate cancer treatments. Private Equity firm Bain Capital Acquires Mitsubishi Tanabe Pharma In a move with significant implications across the industry, the finance giant founded by the now Governor of Utah, Mitt Romney, Bain Capital is set to acquire Mitsubishi Tanabe Pharma , a Japanese drugmaker with roots dating back to 1678​ , in a deal reportedly worth around ¥510 billion (approximately $3.3–$3.4 billion)​. The buyout underscores the rising interest of private equity in life sciences, where investors are drawn to steady revenue from specialty pharmaceuticals (Mitsubishi Tanabe is known for niche drugs, including treatments for rare diseases) and see potential to streamline operations through layoffs, and for growth​ that some critics allege will come from structural price gouging tactics well known to such private equity organizations. Mitsubishi Tanabe’s portfolio spans neurology and immunology (autoimmune disorders), vaccines, and metabolic conditions, and it boasts a global footprint through partnerships in these areas​. Bain has made preliminary statements that it plans to significantly invest in the company’s pipeline through “boosting” R&D and pursuing external collaborations – and may leverage cutting-edge tools like AI-driven drug discovery to enhance innovation​. This acquisition highlights how private capital is increasingly targeting pharma companies with high-margin, specialized therapeutics , with Bain and others betting on long-term value in focused biopharma franchises​. Skeptics fear that the increasing presence of private equity in life science will lead to more characters like Martin Shkreli aka Pharma Bro. Tasso Unveils Home-Based Blood Collection for Remote Trials Seattle-based medical device innovator, Tasso has rolled out a new at-home blood collection device that yields dried blood spot samples, aiming to make decentralized clinical trials more accessible and convenient​.. The kit includes a wearable push-button lancet (the Tasso-Mini) and a Tile-T20 cartridge that together collect a set of precise blood droplets, which dry into spots and can be shipped to a lab via mail and stored at room temperature without special handling. Though it may initially sound trivial, allowing patients to self-collect blood at home instead of traveling for clinic visits helps tackle several remote-trial challenges – from simplifying logistics and scheduling to improving patient compliance and access to timely biomarker sampling in diverse locales​. With the rise of personalized medicine and digital health, at-home sampling solutions like Tasso’s are gaining traction as a patient-friendly way to gather real-world data and make trials more inclusive. Ultimately, Tasso’s device is expected to streamline clinical workflows by reducing patient burden and broadening trial participation beyond major medical centers, hopefully accelerating enrollment and enriching the data collected in studies​. 🗓️ Upcoming BiB Events See our handy dandy Lu.ma event calendar HERE , please RSVP so folks can plan accordingly! title: How to build an effective biotech pitch deck
text: Biotech pitch decks pose a unique challenge. They require a delicate balance between diving deep into the science and meeting your audience where they are. Founders need to translate complex scientific concepts into compelling narratives while ensuring clarity and accessibility for investors with varying levels of technical expertise. At Wunderdogs , we specialize in helping biotech companies craft pitch decks that resonate. From Seed through Series B and beyond, we’ve supported clients by crafting thoughtful narratives and matching them with engaging, accessible designs. Our cumulative experience has helped clients raise over $800MM in funding, giving us a deep understanding of what makes a biotech pitch deck stand out. What makes a biotech pitch deck special Biotech pitch decks often require more depth, detail, and length than those of other industries. This is due to the field’s inherent complexity: biotech products are often technology-intensive, deeply rooted in science, and take much longer to commercialize. Investors need a comprehensive understanding of not only your product’s potential but also the data and validation supporting it. Biotech companies also tend to adopt platform-based approaches, meaning they offer a foundational technology with applications across multiple use cases. This requires presenting a broad vision while maintaining focus on your immediate, actionable steps. Narrative elements of a successful biotech pitch deck Framing and storytelling Much like in any other industry, a compelling narrative is essential for making your pitch memorable. However, biotech investors tend to be very cautious about grand promises and lofty claims. Introduce your big vision early in the deck, capturing the audience’s imagination with the potential of your technology. However, balance this with the practical, tangible steps you’re taking to move forward. Acknowledge the challenges inherent in the industry, such as long commercialization timelines, regulatory hurdles, or essential clinical trials, and show how your strategy mitigates these risks. A narrative that combines ambition with pragmatism is far more likely to gain investor confidence. Science and data Science is the backbone of any biotech pitch, but presenting it effectively is a balancing act. Both overloading and under-explaining the science can alienate your audience. Focus on the most critical elements that support your pitch and weave them into your narrative. Experimental data is another notable cornerstone. The "one slide, one point" rule is invaluable here: use individual slides to emphasize key findings rather than overwhelming your audience with too much data at once. Highlight results that align with your narrative and support your product’s viability. For more technical or detailed information, include a robust appendix or data room that investors can reference later, or that you can pull from during your pitch when needed. Human focus Investors want to see not only that your team has the expertise to develop the technology but also that they understand the challenges of navigating a complex and highly regulated industry. Beyond the standard team and advisor slide, weave your team’s qualifications into the broader story. Highlight their track record, expertise, and strategic thinking throughout the deck. Show how their combined skills make them uniquely positioned to tackle the challenges of bringing your product to market. Spotlight on deck design The design of your pitch deck is just as important as its content. Good design can help transform dense information into a clear, engaging story. Here are some ways in which design can elevate your narrative: Let your visuals tell a story Visuals are one of the most powerful tools in a biotech pitch deck, but they need to be used with intention. Infographics, for instance, should focus on illustrating one key point at a time. Think of each infographic as a chapter in your story. Avoid the temptation to cram multiple concepts into a single graphic. Instead, use white space to draw attention to what matters most and ensure your audience grasps the takeaway at a glance. Design data for impact Biotech pitches rely on experimental data to back up claims, but too much raw data can drown your message. Tables filled with numbers are hard to digest and often fail to make an impression. Instead, consider how you can visualize your data to highlight key insights. Bar charts, line graphs, and pie charts allow you to showcase trends and relationships in a way that’s easy to understand. Pair these visuals with concise labels and annotations to ensure the data connects directly to your narrative. Keep the focus on your story Every design choice should serve your story. Arrange slides in a logical flow, ensuring each one builds on the last. Use section dividers, arrows, or subtle transitions to signal shifts in focus and help your audience follow along. If a slide doesn’t advance your narrative, consider whether it truly belongs in the deck. Engage without overwhelming Finally, aim for simplicity. Slides overloaded with text, visuals, or data can confuse your audience and dilute your message. Instead, embrace the power of white space and let your key points shine. A well-spaced, uncluttered design not only looks professional but also gives your audience room to absorb and reflect on your ideas. Going from Seed to Series A The jump from Seed to Series A is often one of the most challenging transitions for all startups, and biotech is no exception. While other industries focus on proving product-market fit at this stage, biotech founders need to place their product within the larger framework of the industry. Seed-stage decks typically introduce early experimental data and outline a potential application for the technology. By Series A, investors expect to see more extensive data alongside a clearer definition of your product and its role in the industry. This might include regulatory insights, preliminary feedback from potential partners, or further development of your commercialization plan. Investors want to see not only that your science is sound but also that you’re starting to carve out a path toward market integration. Your pitch should address how your product aligns with existing workflows, solves a specific problem, or creates a meaningful opportunity within the industry. For platform products, it’s usually helpful to zero in on a specific application and build on that. By Series A, investors will want to see achievable milestones and an addressable market instead of compelling grand promises. Conclusion In biotech, complexity is the norm, so building a deck requires a deep and thoughtful approach. Balancing science with a clear, compelling narrative is no easy task, but it’s essential for capturing investor attention. By focusing on a strong story and clean, intentional design, you can distill complexity into clarity and ensure your deck resonates. Remember, every slide should serve your narrative and reflect the promise that defines your company. With the right balance of depth and simplicity, your pitch deck can open doors to your company’s future. Want to learn more? Get in touch with Wunderdogs ! Thanks for reading Bits in Bio! title: Bits In Bio Weekly - February 4th 2025
text: 📢 Highlights Owkin, Bioptimus Develop Smaller, Faster AI Model for Digital Pathology Illumina Announces Supply Chain Challenges Amidst President Trump’s Trade War Following Failed Partnership, Affibody Takes Back Control of Key Asset from Acelyrin Not yet a member of our super awesome slack community of >8500? Join HERE 🤗 👀 In Case You Missed it .. Distilling Foundation Models for Robust and Efficient Models in Digital Pathology Research on foundation models for digital pathology is growing for tasks such as biomarker prediction, gene expression analysis, and tissue classification. This new paper by Owkin and Bioptimus teams explores distilling large foundation models to create smaller, more efficient models without sacrificing performance. The authors distilled H-Optimus-0, a model with over 1 billion parameters, into H0-mini, a compact version with 86 million parameters. Despite its reduced size, H0-mini achieved competitive results on benchmarks like HEST and EVA and demonstrated superior robustness to variations in tissue staining and scanning on the PLISM dataset. Thanks to its efficiency and robustness, H0-mini is a strong contender for clinical use, with lower computational costs without compromising performance or reliability in real-world settings. Illumina Faces Challenges Amid U.S.-China Trade Tensions and Tariffs. Finding fellowship amongst myriad industry players whose operations and strategies have been upended by recent regime changes in the White House, the legendary genetic sequencing company Illumina has reported it has been significantly impacted by recent U.S.-China trade tensions , resulting in increased tariffs on its products. The company is navigating these challenges as it continues to provide sequencing technologies globally, while admitting the possibility of distributions, delays, and price changes. The broader implications of these trade dynamics on the biotech industry remain to be seen. Illumina is assessing the potential impact on its supply chain and exploring strategies to mitigate the effects of the tariffs. The situation underscores the complexities global companies face amid shifting international trade policies amongst all industries, including those whose products and services influence life and death decisions on a daily basis. Behind the Deal: Affibody’s Second Act - Seizing Opportunity with Izokibep Affibody recently announced that it has regained the rights to izokibep , an autoimmune drug candidate it once licensed to Acelyrin. The partnership originally sparked high expectations: Acelyrin paid $25 million upfront in 2021, secured $540 million in IPO funding, and pursued izokibep as a potential breakthrough for conditions like hidradenitis suppurativa, a chronic inflammatory skin condition. However, despite meeting primary endpoints in phase 3 trials , Acelyrin halted its investment, citing challenges in capitalizing on the drug’s potential. Acelyrin’s CEO, Mina Kim, suggested the asset required resources and market reach beyond the company’s capacity. This decision illustrates a critical lesson for investors: even promising drugs can falter if the necessary commercial infrastructure or strategic focus is lacking. With recent clinical setbacks in other indications, Acelyrin ultimately chose to return the rights. Now, Affibody sees an opportunity. CEO David Bejker remains optimistic, positioning izokibep as a game-changer for dermatological conditions. With a solid phase 3 data package, Affibody intends to tap into the drug’s potential where Acelyrin left off. By reclaiming izokibep, Affibody signals its intention to reassert itself in the autoimmune space. Whether through new partnerships or an independent path, Affibody’s approach will be closely watched as an indicator of how smaller biotech companies can navigate complex development and commercialization challenges. Recursion Expands AI Application into Clinical Trials Utah-based Recursion Pharmaceuticals is advancing its AI capabilities beyond drug discovery into clinical trial s. Known for its automated and high throughput morphology-based screening platform and the petabytes of data it has produced, the company is turning its attention downstream in the developmental process - with at least seven programs anticipated to enter human trials or yield clinical data in 2025, the company has initiated a "ClinTech" effort. This initiative aims to enhance trial design, expedite patient enrollment, and improve evidence generation. By leveraging AI, Recursion hopes to optimize study protocols, identify suitable patient populations more efficiently, and generate robust clinical evidence, thereby accelerating the development timeline and increasing the likelihood of successful outcomes, “First, it’s really around smarter trial design programs. Second, it’s around accelerating enrollment. And the third is around enhancing evidence generation,” Najat Khan, PhD, Recursion’s chief R&D officer and chief commercial officer. Molecular-driven Foundation Model for Oncologic Pathology Researchers from Harvard Medical School, Massachusetts General Hospital, and the Broad Institute have developed THREADS , a slide-level foundation model that integrates histology images with genomic and transcriptomic data to generate comprehensive whole-slide embeddings. THREADS is trained on over 47,000 tissue sections from 39 organs and captures tissue morphology and molecular profiles. It outperforms models like PRISM and GIGAPATH across 54 oncology tasks, including mutation prediction, treatment response, and survival analysis. THREADS performs well in data-scarce scenarios, offers robust off-the-shelf embeddings for clinical tasks, and supports fine-tuning, making it a versatile tool for computational pathology. In addition to the preprint, a longer form description is available through the authors’ team’s blog post . 8VC Targets $1 Billion Fund to Invest in Tech and Life Sciences The well known Bay-area venture capital firm 8VC, known for backing companies like Altos Labs, is aiming to raise a $1 billion fund focused on technology and life sciences investments. The firm’s 6th funct, the focus of which reflects the firm's commitment to supporting innovative startups at the intersection of technology and biology. The fund is expected to fuel advancements in areas such as biotechnology, healthcare, and computational biology. 8VC's strategy involves identifying and nurturing early-stage companies that leverage technological innovations to address complex challenges in life sciences. The raise is likely to be successful given the VC’s founder, Joe Lonsdale, has built his career on co-founding the defense IT contracting firm Palantir. Astellas Announces Management Restructuring Amid Strong Financial Performance Japan-based biopharmaceutical powerhouse, Astellas Pharma, has initiated a major management restructuring , effective April 1, 2025. To call it a bloodbath may not be inappropriate. Included amongst the sudden seniormost departures: General Counsel Catherine Levitt, chief manufacturing officer Hideki Shima, and Chief Scientific Officer Yoshitsugu Shitaka, Ph.D. Additionally, Chief Medical Officer Tadaaki Taniguchi, M.D., Ph.D., will be moved to a newly created position of chief research and development officer. This move is being reported as a means of streamline operations, increasing efficiency, and enhancing decision-making processes. Normally such changes occur on the backfoot of significant corporate decline, but in this case it is the opposite - Astellas reported a robust nine-month earnings, driven by the expansion of sales of XTANDI™ (enzalutamide) for the treatment of prostate cancer that was approved in 2023. Multi-Modal Transformer Enhances Cell Type-Agnostic Regulatory Predictions A recent study published in Cell Genomics introduces a multi-modal transformer model designed to improve regulatory genome predictions across various cell types. This model integrates multiple data modalities, including genomic sequences and epigenetic information, to predict regulatory elements without being confined to specific cell types. The approach enhances the generalizability and accuracy of regulatory predictions, facilitating a deeper understanding of gene regulation mechanisms across diverse biological contexts. This advancement holds potential for broad applications in genomics and personalized medicine, but its impact will take time to manifest and hard to index to this particular innovation. AdvanCell Secures $112 Million in Series C Funding For Radiopharmaceutical Therapies The Australian radiopharmaceutical company AdvanCell has completed a $112 million Series C . The funding, led by Sanofi Ventures, will support the progression of AdvanCell's lead asset, a targeted alpha-emitting radiotherapy, into clinical trials. This investment represents a significant step in AdvanCell's journey, which started in relative obscurity in 2019, to become a global leader in radionuclide-based cancer therapeutics. Their approach focuses on leveraging alpha-emitting isotopes to deliver targeted radiation therapy, improving efficacy while minimizing damage to surrounding healthy tissues. The company plans to utilize the funds to advance its pipeline and expand its manufacturing capabilities beyond its 40,000 core operating site to meet anticipated demand. Owkin Administers First Dose of Its AI-Optimized Cancer Therapy The French Techbio titan, Owkin, has commenced its INVOKE clinical trial by dosing the first patient with OKN4395, a first-in-class EP2/EP4/DP1 triple inhibitor designed to counteract cancer's immune evasion mechanisms. Developed using Owkin's AI-powered K1.0 operating system, OKN4395 targets prostaglandin receptors that tumors exploit to suppress immune responses. The Phase I study will assess the drug's safety, tolerability, and preliminary anti-tumor activity, both as a monotherapy and in combination with pembrolizumab. This trial marks a significant milestone in applying AI to accelerate the development of novel cancer therapies. Lantern Pharma Launches AI-Driven Platform for Antibody-Drug Conjugate Development The Dallas Texas based, and publicly traded, company Lantern Pharma has announced advancements in its RADR platform, integrating a new AI-powered module designed to accelerate and optimize the development of antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs). The enhanced platform has allegedly identified 82 potential ADC targets, including 22 that are clinically validated, along with 60 novel targets with potential for intellectual property and portfolio expansion. The company additional stated that RADR has screened over 50,000 compounds, validating 729 payload molecules with therapeutic potential. Lantern's approach leverages AI to analyze complex genomic and transcriptomic datasets, enabling the identification of optimal target-payload combinations for ADCs. The company estimates that this AI-driven approach could reduce ADC development timelines by 30–50% and lower costs by up to 60% compared to traditional methods. 🗓️ Upcoming BiB Events See our handy dandy Lu.ma event calendar HERE , please RSVP so folks can plan accordingly! title: Bits In Bio Weekly - January 28th 2025
text: 📢 Highlights Australian-Chinese Team Develops AI Tool, ProtChatGPT,  for Protein Research Retro Biosciences, OpenAI Develop New Model for Longevity-focused Cell Reprogramming Neomorph Adds AbbVie to Growing List of Billion Dollar  Pharma Partnerships Not yet a member of our super awesome slack community of >8500? Join HERE 🤗 👀 In Case You Missed it .. ProtChatGPT: Towards Protein Understanding with Hybrid Representation + LLM's Researchers from the University of Technology Sydney and Zhejiang University have introduced ProtChatGPT , an AI system that connects large language models (LLMs) with protein research. It allows users to upload protein sequences, ask questions, and receive detailed answers. The system incorporates multi-level protein encoding sequence, secondary and tertiary structures processed through a custom PLP-former for accurate information retrieval. The models Protein Context Gating (PCG) module improves structure alignment through contrastive learning, enabling applications such as mutation analysis, disease associations, and protein design. With its open-source code and pre-trained model, ProtChatGPT aims to support protein research and foster scientific collaboration. OpenAI Develops New Model, GPT-4b, for Protein Engineering and Life Extension In a much anticipated move OpenAI has partnered with longevity science biotech Retro Biosciences , a San Francisco-based research company, to develop a model named GPT-4b micro for reprogramming mature cells into stem cells. Scientists at Retro used a prompting approach similar to the “few-shot” method to obtains suggestions for redesigns of Yamanaka proteins. According to the MIT Technology Review, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman is Retro’s biggest investor and personally funded the company to the tune of $180 million. This collaboration marks OpenAI’s entry into the TechBio space and its collaboration with Retro may reveal insights on the potential of applying AI to extending human lifespan by targeting stem cell efficiency. Critics in conventional pharma look at the move as yet another "tech bro” investing in longevity programs that serve themselves and the healthy few that can afford such elective interventions. Behind the Deal: Neomorph Strikes $1.64B Partnership with AbbVie Neomorph has secured a partnership with AbbVie worth up to $1.64 billion in option fees, milestones, and royalties, focusing on molecular glue degraders for oncology and immunology. This marks Neomorph’s third major collaboration since its 2020 founding, following deals with Biogen ($1.45B) and Novo Nordisk ($1.46B). The agreement reflects a growing industry trend, with companies like Novartis and Roche also investing heavily in protein degradation technologies . For AbbVie, this collaboration diversifies its pipeline beyond established immunology blockbusters and positions it at the forefront of an emerging therapeutic approach. Molecular glue degraders represent a novel class of small molecules designed to selectively induce degradation of disease-driving proteins, including those previously considered “undruggable”. This approach could revolutionize treatments in oncology and immunology, addressing high-value targets across multiple therapeutic areas. The deal structure, combining upfront payments with milestones and royalties, highlights Big Pharma’s increasing reliance on external innovation to de-risk development pipelines. It also showcases how platform companies like Neomorph can leverage strategic collaborations to accelerate growth while retaining control over their innovative technologies. Manas AI secures $24.6 Million for AI Cancer-Research In a curious turn of Silicon Valley seeking entrance into biotech, Reid Hoffman, a tech entrepreneur and venture capitalist widely known for co-founding the social media platform LinkedIn, has raised funding for Manas AI , a startup that will use AI for drug discovery and "will first focus on breast cancer, prostate cancer and lymphoma before broadening its scope to include other illnesses." Hoffman is co-founding the startup with Dr. Siddhartha Mukherjee, a physician well-known for his book "The Emperor of All Maladies." Dr. Mukherjee is quoted in the Wall Street Journal saying "he plans for Manas, named after the Sanskrit word for 'mind,' to make money primarily by developing and selling its own drugs." General Catalyst and Greylock Partners invested in the $24.6M funding round and Manas plans to use Microsoft-owned data centers. A Foundation Model of Transcription Across Human Cell Types In a recent publication in Nature , researchers introduced the General Expression Transformer (GET) , a foundation model for transcriptional regulation designed to predict gene expression across 213 human cell types using chromatin accessibility and sequence data. GET achieves experimental-level accuracy in unseen cell types and outperforms previous models in tasks such as regulatory element identification and transcription factor interaction mapping. The model uncovering universal and cell-type-specific regulatory grammars, provides insights into long-range enhancer-promoter interactions and disease-linked genetic variations. This is a significant step toward understanding gene regulation and its implications for health and disease. Novo-Backed Windward Bio Secures $200M in Funding and in-Licenses Biologics Windward Bio, a Switzerland-based biotech backed by Novo Holdings, has raised a $200 million in Series A financing to advance small-molecule oncology programs across multiple indications. Simultaneously, the company announced a partnership worth up to $970 million, paying 45M upfront to license rights to Kelun-Biotech and Harbour BioMed’s clinical-stage monoclonal antibody. The biotech paid the co-developers $45 million in upfront and near-term milestone payments—using both cash and equity interest in Windward’s undisclosed parent company. The financing round was co-led by OrbiMed, Novo Holdings and Blue Owl Healthcare Opportunities. Computational Protein Design for Neutralizing Snake Venom Toxins Researchers at the Technical University of Denmark in Lyngby and at the Baker Lab at the University of Washington have published a paper which highlights the use of artificial intelligence for effectively neutralizing deadly snake venom toxins. The paper highlights the high cost and limited efficacy of polyclonal antibodies derived from the plasma of immunized animals against three-finger toxins (3FTx). They used deep learning methods for generating de novo proteins which demonstrate “remarkable thermal stability, high binding affinity and near-atomic-level agreement with the computational models.” This breakthrough could lead to safer, more accessible, and cost-effective antivenom treatments, particularly beneficial for neglected tropical diseases. Data is available on the PDB and code explanation and examples of binder design using RFDiffusion are available on Github. Basetwo's $115M Raise Aims to Transform Pharma Production with Applied AI Toronto based pioneer in AI-driven pharmaceutical manufacturing, Basetwo, has raised $115 million in Series B funding to scale its first-principles optimization platform . The company’s technology integrates machine learning with physics-based models to streamline bioprocessing, enhance yield predictability, and minimize batch failures. Investors, including Coatue and a16z, see Basetwo’s platform as a key enabler for biopharma companies seeking to reduce production costs and improve supply chain robustness. The funding will accelerate deployments with leading pharma manufacturers and expand applications beyond biologics to small-molecule drugs and cell therapies into emerging modalities. Mayo Clinic Teams with NVIDIA to Advance AI in Pathology NVIDIA and clinical care giant Mayo Clinic have launched a strategic collaboration to integrate AI-powered digital pathology into clinical workflows. Using NVIDIA’s DGX Cloud and MONAI framework, the partnership will develop deep learning models for cancer diagnostics, biomarker discovery, and pathology automation. The collaboration will leverage Mayo's vast data stores, produced through manual and robotic lab work- their database includes 10 million patient records and 20 million high-resolution slide images. The AI models will analyze whole-slide images to identify patterns that human pathologists might miss, enhancing diagnostic accuracy and reducing turnaround time. With many parallel efforts underway in both institutional and corporate settings, time will tell if their efforts will prove meaningfully differentiated from the like of Microsoft's GigaPath . Quantum-Computing-Enhanced Algorithm Unveils Potential KRAS Inhibitors An academic-industry partnership comprising the University of Toronto, Insilico Medicine, and other institutions have published research on the use of quantum computing for the development of KRAS inhibitors for cancer therapy. The paper highlights the use of a “hybrid quantum-classical model” which involves utilizing a 16-qubit quantum computer alongside traditional machine learning to generate and evaluate new drug candidates. They identified 15 promising compounds for synthesis and experimental testing. Insilico Medicine provided the initial dataset of 650 experimental ligands and the group used computational screening methods to increase the size of the training set for the generative model. Datasets and code for running the generative model are available via Zenodo. AI Veterinary Startup Lupa Nets $4M to Transform Pet Healthcare London-based Lupa, an AI-driven veterinary health platform, has raised $4 million in seed funding to enhance diagnostics and personalized pet healthcare. The company utilizes AI to analyze clinical data, detect early disease markers, and recommend tailored treatment plans for pets. Lupa’s platform integrates electronic health records and imaging data to assist veterinarians in making data-driven decisions, reducing misdiagnoses and improving pet outcomes - very much like the human equivalents seek to do in conventional healthcare settings. The funding, led by early-stage investors including firstminute capital, 2100 Ventures , and Exor Venture s, will be used to expand the platform’s capabilities and validate its offerings prior to a full go to market. 🗓️ Upcoming BiB Events See our handy dandy Lu.ma event calendar HERE , please RSVP so folks can plan accordingly!
