title: Bits In Bio Weekly - September 24th 2024
text: 📢 Highlights Genentech open sources gReLU for AI-enabled DNA Sequence Analysis Novo Nordisk launches "Golden Ticket Program” to support biotech startups COVID-19 mRNA vaccines appear to enhance cancer Immunotherapies Not yet a member of our super awesome slack community of >7500? Join HERE 🤗 👀 In Case You Missed it .. gReLU: A Comprehensive Framework for DNA Sequence Modeling and Design Scientists at SF-based Genentech have introduced gReLU , a comprehensive deep learning framework coded in Python aimed at streamlining DNA sequence modeling for biomedical research use cases and applications. Available on GitHub , the new package is provided as a unified toolkit for each steps of DNA sequence modeling: data processing, model training, evaluation, interpretation, variant effect prediction, and ML-guided DNA design. It also provides a "model zoo" with pre-trained models that can be fine-tuned by a user's unique data. The framework aims to bridge the gap between diverse models and software, enhancing interoperability in DNA sequence modeling. One example of its utility is in modeling chromatin accessibility and predicting non-coding variant effects, such as enhancers and promoters . The team hopes to iterate and improve the tool by adding masked language models, additional sequence design algorithms and efficient training on larger multi-species datasets. Flagship Pioneering's Generate Biomedicine Announces $1B Novartis Collaboration Boston based Generate Biomedicines has teamed up with Novartis in a multi-target collaboration for using AI to design a variety of novel protein therapies as well as enzymes and cell therapies.. The partnership could result in milestone payments exceeding $1 billion with $65 million upfront. Founded by Flagship in 2018, Generate is no stranger to large deals - booking over $600M in financing in its last two raises, and inking an agreement worth up to $1.9 billion biobucks in 2022  with Amgen to develop five initial programs. Insilico’s AI Drug for IPF Shows Promising Phase IIa Results One of the founding ventures in bio AI, Insilico Medicine has reported positive Phase IIa results for ISM001-055, an AI-designed TNIK inhibito r aimed at treating idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF). The drug demonstrated safety and dose-dependent improvements in lung function, marking a significant milestone for AI-driven drug discovery. The study represents a step forward in using AI to design drugs for complex diseases like IPF, where effective treatments have been limited. With these promising results, ISM001-055 is being touted as potentially the first AI-discovered drug to enter late-stage clinical development for IPF. Broad's LEGO Enables Modified Messenger RNAs with Enhanced Function The Broad Institute has introduced a new mRNA framework known as Ligation-Enhanced GOod mRNA or LEGO, to unlock new treatments for a wide array of diseases by engineering multiple "caps" and "tails" on mRNA molecules—key regions that regulate how long the mRNA lasts and how efficiently it produces proteins. This could mean more effective vaccines, fewer side effects, better treatments for conditions like diabetes or anemia, and even improved COVID-19 vaccine responses. Leveraging chemotopological engineering, where multiple caps create a synergistic effect, the LEGO framework has the potential to fine-tune mRNA therapies for various medical applications. It has already shown promise  in mice, enhancing SARS-CoV-2 vaccine efficacy. George Church’s GCTx Raises $75M for Advanced Cell Therapy GC Therapeutics (GCTx), a startup originating from George Church’s lab, has raised $75 million to accelerate its cell therapy platform, TFome , which dramatically shortens the time needed to produce induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs). The company’s innovative approach could cut the time for developing iPSCs by up to 100 times, offering significant advantages for disease treatments. With $65 million from a recent Series A, GCTx aims to revolutionize regenerative medicine and cell-based therapies. The platform is expected to "transform cell therapy pipelines for a wide range of diseases, including cancer and neurodegenerative disorders." Do COVID-19 mRNA Vaccines Boost Cancer Immunotherapy? In a surprising development, a study presented at the European Society for Medical Oncology (ESMO) annual meeting indicates that COVID-19 mRNA vaccines may enhance the effectiveness of cancer immunotherapies , particularly checkpoint inhibitors such as the well known biologic, Keytruda. The research suggests that these vaccines can boost the immune system’s response against tumors by activating a stronger immune reaction. Moderna and Pfizer, key players in mRNA technology, may find these findings relevant, though the exact mechanisms remain unclear and require further investigation. This discovery opens new possibilities for combining mRNA vaccines with existing cancer treatments, though more clinical trials are needed to confirm its safety and efficacy. Nomic Bio Raises $42M to Expand Protein Profiling Platform Montreal-based Nomic Bio has secured $42 million in Series B funding to scale its nELISA protein profiling platform. This oversubscribed round will accelerate the company’s commercial operations and meet the growing demand from biotech and pharma companies for high-throughput proteomics. Nomic’s platform enables researchers to measure thousands of proteins in parallel, improving drug discovery and development workflows. The technology offers a new level of precision in protein interaction data, a crucial aspect for modern drug discovery. Leadership Crisis Hits 23andMe as 7 Board Members Resign Following a steep decline in public equity value and a failed bid by CEO Anne Wojcicki to take the company private, all seven independent board members of 23andMe have resigned . This mass resignation marks a major governance crisis for the direct-to-consumer genetic testing pioneer, which has faced mounting challenges due to falling demand for its services and increased competition. Despite 23andMe’s early role in revolutionizing personalized genetics, its stock price has plummeted by over 90% since going public through a SPAC. The company now faces an uncertain future, as it grapples with a strategy to recover from its downward trajectory. AI-Powered Portal for Cancer Clinical Trial Access by Massive Bio Massive Bio has launched the "Patient Connect" platform, an AI-powered portal that matches cancer patients to clinical trials tailored to their medical profiles. The tool enhances access to cutting-edge cancer therapies by identifying eligible trials, offering concierge services, and connecting patients with a clinical support network. This initiative addresses a crucial gap in cancer treatment by improving patient recruitment for clinical trials, which often face challenges in finding suitable participants. Massive Bio’s AI solution "helps eliminate the barriers to clinical trial enrollment, making life-saving therapies more accessible to cancer patients globally." Novo Nordisk Supports Early Biotech Innovation with "Golden Ticket" Pioneer Group and Novo Nordisk have teamed up to launch t he Golden Ticket Programme , offering early-stage biotech companies 12 months of rent-free lab space, access to Pioneer’s venture programs, and expert mentoring from Novo Nordisk. This initiative aims to support breakthrough research in cardiometabolic health, diabetes, and rare diseases by fostering collaboration between industry experts and emerging biotech startups. With the application deadline set for November 15, 2024, the program is designed to propel early-stage innovations forward. The Golden Ticket winners will also gain "access to global R&D opportunities through Novo Nordisk’s extensive research network." Constructive Bio Raises $58M to Advance Synthetic Biology The UK-based biotech, Constructive Bio, has secured $58 million in funding to enhance its synthetic biology platform, which focuses on rewriting genomes and engineering cell systems for new molecules. This funding round, led by Blue Horizon, supports the development of programmable biomolecules for a range of industries, including pharmaceuticals and agriculture. The company's board has gained significant expertise with the addition of Nobel laureate Venki Ramakrishnan. Constructive Bio’s platform has the potential to revolutionize the way we manufacture complex molecules across different sectors. WhiteLab to Collaborate With Debiopharma for AI-Enhanced Cancer Drug Delivery Debiopharm has partnered with WhiteLab Genomics to leverage AI in optimizing lipid nanoparticle (LNP) delivery for oncology treatments. By identifying cancer-specific receptors and binding agents, the collaboration aims to improve the targeting and effectiveness of cancer therapies. This partnership will enable more precise drug delivery, increasing treatment specificity and reducing side effects. WhiteLab’s AI-driven platform will "help identify new biomarkers for enhancing the precision of cancer drug delivery." BRD4 Inhibitor from DeepCure Shows Preclinical Efficacy for MAS DeepCure will present promising preclinical data on its BRD4 (BD2) inhibitor, DC-9476, at the EMBO 2024 Conference. The drug demonstrated significant efficacy in treating macrophage activation syndrome (MAS), outperforming standard glucocorticoid therapy in preclinical models. DC-9476 works by directly inhibiting macrophage activation, a crucial driver of MAS, which has limited treatment options. The preclinical results highlight the potential for DC-9476 to address unmet medical needs in inflammatory diseases like MAS, which is currently bereft of effective and available therapeutics interventions. Variational AI and Rakovina Therapeutics Parts for AI-enabled Cancer Drug Discovery Rakovina Therapeutics has announced a collaboration with Variational AI to develop small-molecule therapies targeting DNA-damage response (DDR) kinases. Using Variational AI’s Enki™ platform, the partnership will focus on identifying drug candidates for cancer treatments that address DDR-related mechanisms. The collaboration is set to accelerate the drug discovery process, providing new therapeutic options for difficult-to-treat cancers. The Enki™ platform "has shown promise in identifying molecules with superior drug-like properties for oncology applications." 🗓️ Upcoming BiB Events See our handy dandy Lu.ma event calendar HERE , please RSVP so folks can plan accordingly! title: Bits In Bio Weekly - September 17th 2024
text: 📢 Highlights Welcome ‘Chai-A’, the new protein design foundation model on the block ProteinBench, evaluation framework for protein models, has been released Think AI company spend is high? Moderna's $4.5B R&D raises eyebrows Not yet a member of our super awesome slack community of >7500? Join HERE 🤗 👀 In Case You Missed it .. Chai Discovery Enters the Protein Modeling Race with Chai-1 The race to reproduce Alphafold3 continues! A San Francisco based startup, Chai discovery,  has emerged from stealth mode with the introduction of Chai-A, its proprietary multi-modal protein foundation model .  In contrast to many bioai foundation AI companies, Chai's model is open-source for non-commercial use, allowing further research and development. The company, founded by former OpenAI and Meta researchers, recently raised nearly $30 million in a seed funding round led by Thrive Capital and OpenAI, valuing the company at $150 million. What sets Chai-1 apart from other tools, like Google DeepMind’s AlphaFold, is the fact that it leverages the protein language model ESM built by Meta to  to build protein sequence embeddings represented purely as text, bypassing the MSA (Multi Sequence Alignment) step, an albeit time-consuming step of AlphaFold2 that nonetheless has been responsible for its revolutionary performance.  On top of sharing the mode itself, the team also p roduced a detailed technical report which provides nuanced exploration of their methodology and discussion of their results.. New Research shows COVID-19 mRNA Vaccines  Boost Cancer Therapies. In a surprising development, a study presented at the European Society for Medical Oncology (ESMO) annual meeting indicates that COVID-19 mRNA vaccines may enhance the effectiveness of cancer immunotherapies , particularly checkpoint inhibitors such as the well known biologic, Keytruda. The research suggests that these vaccines can boost the immune system’s response against tumors by activating a stronger immune reaction. Moderna and Pfizer, key players in mRNA technology, may find these findings relevant, though the exact mechanisms remain unclear and require further investigation. This discovery opens new possibilities for combining mRNA vaccines with existing cancer treatments, though more clinical trials are needed to confirm its safety and efficacy. Gilead Inks $35M AI Drug Discovery Deal with Genesis Therapeutics Genesis Therapeutics has closed a $35M deal with Gilead , allowing the pharma powerhouse to utilize its GEMS AI platform for drug discovery across three targets. The collaboration may expand, offering Gilead development and commercialization rights, while Genesis could receive milestone payments and royalties. This partnership aligns with Gilead’s strategy to enhance its research pipeline through AI-driven technology. Gilead is targeting complex disease areas with these efforts, including inflammation and cancer rather than their world-leading line of vaccines. ProteinBench: A Holistic Evaluation of Protein Foundation Models Amid the growing efforts to build protein foundation models, Beingjing-based tech conglomerate ByteDance's research team has introduced ProteinBench , a comprehensive open evaluation platform. This platform aims to standardize benchmarking for various tasks such as structure prediction, complex modeling, and design across different models. Through this platform, the team evaluated well-known models like AlphaFold and RosettaFold, revealing their strengths and limitations. The evaluation is structured around four dimensions: quality, novelty, diversity, and robustness. Additionally, the public leaderboard and open-source code foster transparency and collaboration within the research community. CardiaTec Raises $6.5M to Tackle Cardiovascular Disease Cambridge University spinout CardiaTec secured $6.5M in seed funding to develop AI-driven treatments for cardiovascular disease (CVD), the world's leading cause of death, resulting in 17.9 million deaths annually, according to the World Health Organization (WHO) . CardiaTec, founded in 2021, is working with 65 hospitals to build the largest multi-omics heart tissue dataset to accelerate drug discovery. The platform will integrate genomics, transcriptomics, and proteomics to identify novel therapeutic targets. Segmed Raises $10.4M to Expand AI Medical Imaging Platform Led by iGan Partners and Advocate Health, 13 year old Segmed has raised $10.4M in Series A funding to enhance its AI-driven medical imaging platform, with a focus on pathology diagnostic images referred to has IHC (immunohistochemistry) slides. Interestingly, the platform integrates diverse patient data along with its analysis, providing a more streamlined integration and user experience. The platform claims to de-identify and standardize diverse imaging data, enabling faster access for biopharma and AI research applications. With the new funding, Segmed will expand its capabilities and scale its offerings to meet increasing demand for its products, which are already on market generating revenue with a loyal customer base. UK's First Robotic Genomic Testing Facility Launches The Royal Marsden NHS Foundation Trust, in partnership with Automata Technologies, launched the UK’s first robotic genomic testing facility for cancer patients. The facility uses Automata's LINQ platform, automating sample processing with six robotic arms, effectively doubling the hospital's genomic testing capacity. This cutting-edge facility will enable faster and more accurate personalized cancer diagnostics. The increased capacity will reduce turnaround time for critical test results. Though impressive in the context of the UK, several marquee testing facilities, including ARUP and LabCorp, in the US have long ago implemented massive integrated automatic sample processing facilities. Human Longevity Raises $39.8M for AI-Powered Longevity Care Launched by genetics legend Craig Venter and Peter Diamandis, Human Longevity, Inc. raised $39.8M in Series B funding led by TVM Capital Healthcare to expand its Precision 100+ Longevity Care Program. This program integrates whole-genome sequencing, AI health risk assessments, and personalized interventions to reduce biological aging. With the additional funding, the company will enhance its precision health platform to extend healthspan and improve outcomes for age-related diseases for a growing user base both internationally and abroad. Insilico Touts New Preclinical Candidate WRN Inhibitor as 'Promising' One of the world's first Bio AI enterprises, Insilico Medicine, has announced ISM2196, a WRN inhibitor purportedly developed using one or more internal AI systems to target MSI-H metastatic cancers. There is no clinical evidence behind the drug, casting doubt on its merit. Undisclosed preclinical data allegedly shows 'strong anti-tumor activity and drug-like properties'. Insilico hopes to position ISM2196 as a promising treatment for colorectal, endometrial, and gastric cancers. The WRN pathway is critical in DNA repair, and inhibiting it in tumor cells could enhance cancer treatment outcomes. The company plans to further validate its efficacy in additional preclinical studies and clinical trials, while the industry at large continues to be nonplussed by the ratio of press releases relative to drugs making an impact in the clinic. ThinkCyte Secures $32M for AI-Powered Cell Sorting Platform In a development greeted with delight by many single cell geneticists, ThinkCyte raised $32M in Series C funding to drive the global expansion of VisionSort, their AI-based dual-mode fluorescence and morphometric cell sorting platform. This round brings the total funding to $91M, enabling ThinkCyte to scale its technology for use in research and clinical applications. VisionSort offers high-throughput, label-free cell sorting, providing precision in various cell biology studies that was currently not available given the requirement for the introduction of fluorophores. Ginkgo Bioworks Launches Automation Product Line for Labs The problem child of the bioeconomy, Ginkgo Bioworks has launched its new Ginkgo Automation product line , featuring modular tools like Reconfigurable Automation Carts (RACs) and Automation Control Software (ACS). These tools aim to streamline laboratory R&D with low-touch, data-rich solutions for accelerated research and development and support the embattled public companies attempt to re-invent itself after a 95% fall in value and an evaporation of the conviction that led to nearly $1B of investment. Ginkgo’s goal is to offer a flexible, scalable system that integrates seamlessly with existing workflows, providing a 'data factory' - functionally a contract research organization (CRO, leveraging the massive investments made in equipment and systems over its 16 years of operation. F2G Secures $100M for Olorofim Antifungal Development Following an initial failed commercialization of their novel antifungal treatment, the embattled UK-based biotech, F2G, has raised $100M to re-advance its antifungal drug Olorofim , following FDA setbacks. Olorofim belongs to the orotomide class, which inhibits pyrimidine synthesis essential to fungi. The funding will support the drug's development for invasive aspergillosis, with commercialization in the U.S. targeted for next year. This drug could address a critical unmet need in antifungal treatments as resistance rises, which has largely been neglected due to its modest market size relative to immunotherapies and anti-cancer therapeutics. AI Device Successfully Treats Life-Threatening Blood Clot A 28-year-old man with acute pulmonary thromboembolism was successfully treated using an AI-guided cardiovascular medical device at Sir Gangaram Hospital in India. The device precisely removed the blood clot with minimal blood loss, avoiding the risks of severe bleeding associated with traditional treatments. This breakthrough demonstrates the potential of AI in improving the safety and efficacy of life-saving medical procedures, showcasing a glimpse of the future where AI is now playing a key role in advancing minimally invasive treatments in critical care settings, domestically and internationally. CytoTronics Raises $13.5M to Advance Live Cell Analysis Boston-based biotech CytoTronics has secured $13.5M in "Seed 2" funding , led by LYFE Capital, to bring its Pixel™ live cell analysis platform to market. Investors have raised an eyebrow at the curious representation of what appears to be a bridge round, normally considered a bearish signal for future investment. The Pixel™ platform offers single-cell resolution for various cell biology applications, allowing researchers to perform high-resolution, real-time analysis of living cells. The funding will accelerate the development and commercialization of the technology, though the timeline for its arrival to the market remains unclear. 4.5B of Spending at Moderna Raises Investor Concern Moderna is facing investor backlash over its aggressive $4.5B annual R&D spend, considered prodigious given consistently declining Covid vaccine sales. Shareholders are urging the biotech to cut back its 45-product pipeline , particularly in less promising areas like rare diseases and HIV. Analysts suggest that Moderna refocus its efforts on high-revenue areas like oncology and infectious diseases. 🗓️ Upcoming BiB Events See our handy dandy Lu.ma event calendar HERE , please RSVP so folks can plan accordingly! title: Bits In Bio Weekly - September 10th 2024
text: 📢 Highlights Deepmind expands Bio AI portfolio with Protein Design tool, AlphaProteo Biotech investing remains strong with over $1B in deals in recent weeks We interview Co-Founder of Cradle.Bio , Elise De Reus PhD, about her journey and the exciting work at Cradle - find it HERE Not yet a member of our super awesome slack community of >7500? Join HERE 🤗 👀 In Case You Missed it .. Google DeepMind Launches AlphaProteo: AI for Protein Design The original team behind AlphaFold, Google's DeepMind has introduced AlphaProteo , an AI model aimed at generating proteins for uses in drug development, biosensors, and disease research. The system shows promising results with higher experimental success rates than other methods on 7 in-vitro tested proteins. It is capable of generating new protein binders for various target proteins, including VEGF-A, which is linked to cancer and diabetes-related complications. Remarkably, this is the first time an AI tool has successfully designed a protein binder specifically for VEGF-A. This new addition to the AlphaFold family seems like a natural progression from protein folding. As AlphaProteo evolves, the team also aims to collaborate with the scientific community on biology problems, as well as explore drug design at Isomorphic Labs. Eli Lilly Bets $409M on AI-Focused Drug Discovery Genetic leap has reached a significant milestone, signing a $409 million deal with Eli Lilly to leverage their proprietary AI platform for drug discovery, particularly in the realm of RNA therapeutics. Consisting of an undisclosed upfront payment and tiered earn out terms, the collaboration will explore advanced genetic medicine technologies, targeting various disease areas. Such genetic medicines, which target the genetic cause of a disease, make up more than 25% of drug candidates being developed at Lilly, the drugmaker's website showed.This move reflects a broader trend of pharmaceutical companies investing heavily in AI to accelerate the development of transformative treatments beyond small molecule and biologics. Relay Therapeutics Reports Early Success in Breast Cancer Drug Trial A biotech company unique in many ways, including its leverage of high performance computing based methods long before their broader industry adoption, Relay Therapeutics has announced promising early results from its Phase 1 trial for RLY-4008, a selective FGFR2 inhibitor aimed at treating FGFR2-altered breast cancer. The trial has shown a favorable safety profile and early signs of clinical efficacy, particularly in patients with advanced, metastatic disease. RLY-4008 is designed to offer better selectivity compared to earlier FGFR inhibitors, potentially minimizing off-target effects. The positive trial results have placed Relay's market valuation back above $1 billion. In addition to its plans for RLY-2608, the company is seeking a commercialization partner for its most advanced drug candidate, which targets bile duct cancer. $325M for CAR-T Advancements from ArsenalBio ArsenalBio secured $325 million in Series C funding to advance its CAR-T therapies for solid tumors, particularly targeting kidney and prostate cancers. The company's lead asset, AB-2100, entered a Phase 1/2 trial for renal cell carcinoma and received FDA fast-track designation. Investors include ARCH Venture Partners, Regeneron, and NVIDIA’s NVentures. The oversubscribed series C fundraise includes new investors Regeneron Ventures, Arch Venture Partners and NVIDIA’s NVentures, among others, according to a Sept. 4 release. The financing round is one of the largest private biotech financings of the year, coming up behind Xaira Therapeutics' $1 billion, Mirador Therapeutics’ $400 million and Formation Bio’s $372 million. As a part of the deal ArsenalBio will prioritize programs aimed at kidney and prostate cancers, while backburning former lead asset AB-1015, a CAR T-cell therapy being studied in a phase 1 ovarian cancer trial. CDD Launches AI Chatbot for Drug Discovery Platform The tried and trusted 22 year old software provider, Collaborative Drug Discovery (CDD), has introduced an AI-powered Support ChatBot for its CDD Vault platform . The new feature enhances user experience by providing instant 24/7 assistance with common inquiries, while complex issues continue to be handled by human support teams. This move aims to streamline customer service and improve response times without compromising on data privacy, as the ChatBot operates without accessing user data. CDD continues to expand its support capabilities in line with the evolving needs of drug discovery, with the development of a chatbot best practices becoming conventionally accepted. BioAge Labs Aims for IPO with Focus on Aging and Metabolic Diseases Longevity-focused BioAge Labs has filed for an IPO , aiming to fund the continued development of its pipeline targeting aging and metabolic diseases. The decade old company is advancing multiple drug candidates designed to extend healthy lifespan by targeting key aging pathways. Their lead candidate, BGE-117, is currently in Phase 2 trials for anemia and muscle weakness in the elderly. The company is also advancing azelaprag through multiple clinical trials. In the ongoing STRIDES Phase 2 trial, azelaprag is being tested in combination with tirzepatide (marketed as Zepbound by Eli Lilly) in approximately 220 older adults with obesity. In parallel, it is also developing therapeutics targeting neuroinflammation. Candid Therapeutics Launches with $370M Series A The most recent venture from biotech veteran Dr. Ken Song, Candid Therapeutics, has debuted with a $370 million Series A , marking one of the largest A rounds in biotech this year. The funding, co-led by Venrock Healthcare, Fairmount, and venBio Partners, aims to position the company as a leader in T-cell engagers for cancer immunotherapies In an unusual move, Candid’s launch combines fundraising and the acquisition of two biotechs, Vignette Bio and TRC 2004, along with their lead assets. Providing the company a robust and defensible foundation in both platform and pipeline. Candid plans to use the remaining funds to drive clinical trials, develop its lead assets, and advance undiscloves next-gen T-cell therapies. The sizable Series A underscores a strong belief in Candid’s ability to revolutionize cancer treatment and continued investor confidence in AI-enabled drug development. LG AI Research Introduces EXAONEPath for Histopathology A relative newcomer to the field of bio AI, Korea-based conglomerate LG has unveiled EXAONEPath, a specialized AI model for analyzing histopathology images . The model uses self-supervised learning to process gigapixel-scale medical images. Such histopathological images can be as large as 40,000 x 40,000 pixels, making it impractical to process them in the same way as general-purpose images. To address this, EXAONEPath utilizes a Multi-Instance Learning (MIL) framework. MIL divides the massive images into smaller patches, each of which is analyzed separately before being integrated into a comprehensive understanding of the entire slide, enabling more precise analysis in fields like cancer diagnostics. This innovation is expected to significantly enhance the efficiency of medical image interpretation and assist pathologists in diagnosing complex cases. Despite its relatively small size of 86 million parameters, EXAONEPath has shown competitive performance across various benchmarks, particularly in predicting microsatellite instability (MSI) in colorectal and stomach cancers. Superluminal Medicines Secures $120M to Target GPCRs Summary Boston based 'generative biology' company, Superluminal Medicines raised $120 million in a Series A to develop drugs targeting G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) for therapeutic development, fresh on the heels of its $33 million seed . This most recent round was led by RA Capital Management, with participation from NVIDIA’s NVentures and Eli Lilly, the proceeds fom which will fuel the company's advancement of 6 therapeutic candidates. With over 30% of drugs targeting GPCRs, one of the more challenging targets in human biology, Superluminal plans to leverage its AI platform to accelerate drug discovery in this vital space. It's platform is designed to analyze the many forms that proteins can take to identify specific disease states mediated by these proteins using machine learning and generative biology to identify new drugs rapidly, hence it's name: “Superluminal” is a term used for certain galaxies and other celestial phenomena that appear to move faster than the speed of light. $67M Series A for 'Protonophores' to Compliment GLP-1's for Obesity, OrsoBio, a palo alot based clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company focusing on obesity treatments, has secured $67 million in its Series B financing round . The funding will accelerate the development of their mitochondrial protonophore portfolio, including TLC-6740, which is currently in Phase 1b clinical trials for obesity treatment. OrsoBio's approach aims to complement GLP-1 receptor agonists by increasing energy expenditure, preserving lean mass, and improving insulin sensitivity. The company is also advancing other compounds, including an ACC2 inhibitor for type 2 diabetes and an LXR inverse agonist for severe hypertriglyceridemia and metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH). As GLP-1 drugs generate billions of revenue for pharmaceutical companies, many new biotechs are in a race to participate in the gold rush. NextRNA Partners with Bayer o Target Long Non-Coding RNA in Cancer Pharma giant Bayer has partnered with NextRNA Therapeutics to develop small-molecule drugs targeting long non-coding RNA, a promising new frontier in cancer treatment. The partnership could be worth over $547 million, with two preclinical programs already underway. By focusing on long non-coding RNA, Bayer and NextRNA aim to address high unmet medical needs in oncology, targeting cancers that have proven difficult to treat with conventional therapies. This collaboration highlights the growing interest in RNA-based therapeutics. Circle Pharma Raises $90M for Macrocycle Drug Development San Francisco based Circle Pharma completed a $90 million Series D round to fund the clinical development of CID-078, a macrocycle drug targeting cyclin A/B in solid tumors. The funding will also support the company’s broader preclinical pipeline, focused on developing cell-permeable macrocycles. Investors include The Column Group, Nextech Invest, and Euclidean Capital. CID-078 is positioned to offer new therapeutic approaches in cancer treatment, with ongoing trials expected to yield promising results. 🎙️ Interview highlight: Elise De Reus PhD of Cradle.Bio "What was really helpful in our discovery phase was falling in love with the problem, not the solution. We looked at the challenges in the biotech field and the capabilities of machine learning, seeking to explore the intersection of these fields through an awesome and highly-usable digital product." title: Bits In Bio Interview: Elise De Reus PhD, Co-Founder Cradle.bio
text: We are thrilled to kick off  the first entry in our new interview series with the brilliant Elise De Reus PhD cofounder of Cradle.Bio Q: Can you give us a quick overview of Cradle and what problem you're solving? A: At Cradle, we're addressing a fundamental challenge in protein design. Traditionally, this field has relied heavily on trial and error, which, given the vast design space, leads to long development timelines and high failure rates. We're building a software platform that puts generative AI at the fingertips of pharma an non-pharma R&D teams, helping them hit their development goals faster. Our platform is designed to work for multiple stakeholders, from associates to scientists, and it gets better with use. We've partnered with a wide range of organizations, from industry giants like J&J and Novo to earlier-stage startups. Importantly, we use a flat-fee billing model rather than charging royalties, as we aim to be part of the broad tech stack and empower as many people as possible across the industry. Q: That's fascinating. Can you tell us a bit about the founding journey of Cradle? A: Certainly. We started Cradle in October 2021. My co-founder Harman and I came from high throughput experimentation at Zymergen and DSM, while our other co-founders, Stef and Eli, brought their machine learning expertise from Google. Our fifth co-founder, Jelle, is exceptional at building usable software. What was really helpful in our discovery phase was falling in love with the problem, not the solution. We looked at the challenges in the biotech field and the capabilities of machine learning, seeking to explore the intersection of these fields through an awesome and highly-usable digital product. Q: Where does Cradle fit within the life sciences ecosystem? A: If you think of the drug development pipeline, we're not involved in the initial target identification phase. Where we really shine is in the discovery and optimization phase, particularly in hit-to-lead identification and lead optimization. This is where drug companies typically spend the most money, and where time is critical. We help our customers develop better candidates in less time to increase chances of success in the preclinical stage, where there are typically only a small number of candidates in the running, and beyond. In the synthetic biology space, we're working with companies who use the Cradle Platform for a range of different applications. Often to engineer better enzymes, moving beyond starting points discovered in nature. Customers who use the Cradle platform test the ML-generated protein designs in in-house wet lab experiments, or at a CRO or cloud lab of their choice. Cradle can be seen as the ML-guided protein design tool in the biotech stack, which would be used alongside ELN and LIMS software, DNA synthesis vendors, and other service providers. Q: It sounds like you've done a great job blending biology and AI. How has this interdisciplinary approach benefited Cradle? A: It's been incredibly valuable. Having expertise in both areas helps the two sides of the biotech coin understand each other better. An important thing to note is that we invested in an in-house wet lab right from Cradle’s start, which allows us to test protein designs from our ML models at a very quick turnaround time. This helps the Cradle team appreciate the value and complexity of generating high quality wet lab data. Our biology team runs a wide variety of test projects, paying close attention to controls and test cases that allow us to build robust models. Conversely, our ML team gains insights into the nuances of biological data generation. It allows them to build the Cradle Platform in such a way that it can deal with things like batch-to-batch variation, which might be caused by differences between operators or switching out a buffer. An interesting feature of our system is that it allows direct expert input into the models, essentially encoding human intelligence. This helps users derive new scientific hypotheses through iteration with the algorithms. It's really a conversation between human expertise and machine learning. Q: Where do you see the field of TechBio evolving? A: I believe we're in an early adopter phase when it comes to utilizing AI in biotech R&D. There's a lot of FOMO - many companies are trying to figure out what they should or could do with AI in biotech. This often involves make-or-buy decisions; do you invest in hiring an ML engineer, explore commercial offerings, or both? At Cradle we're doing a lot of education around the potential applications as well as the ways to think about this decision. I expect protein AI models - both predictive and generative - to become more mainstream in the coming years. The field will need to continue testing different models in different applications, and observing what drives value in individual workflows. For example, in some R&D workflows it is still common to start with screening a large library (eg. an immunization campaign, or a site saturation mutagenesis library). But we’re moving towards a world where generative models can supplement or even replace that phase. I'm also excited about the potential for easier 'prototyping' of early chemical product ideas. Imagine designing a protein, outsourcing data generation, and then doing a super lean small-scale test reactor run. Cloud labs can play a big role here too. Q: What are you most excited about in your work? A: What I love most is the visibility I get into the huge array of problems people are working on. It's truly inspiring to see what can be done with biology - from new approaches to curing cancer to reducing climate impact and ensuring equitable drug access. I'm excited about driving down the cost of early research phases and making it easier to ideate and prototype bio programs. We're seeing benchtop DNA synthesizers becoming rapidly faster and cheaper. Combining these with better design tools and multi-modal models will accelerate the entire industry. I'm particularly excited about advancements in vaccines and biopreparedness. The speed of response and potential for equitable access in this area are really compelling. Q: Finally, what advice would you give to those looking to enter the TechBio field? A: I'd say commit to developing expertise in a specific area, whether it's biotech, AI, or another relevant field. Pursue deep knowledge without necessarily knowing where it will lead. Then, look up and out to see where your contribution can make the most impact. At Cradle, we value what we call the "Humble Steward, Generous Teacher, Curious Student" approach. It's about being open to learning from others while also sharing your own knowledge. Lastly, I'd encourage people to engage with thought leaders in the industry. There are some great blogs and newsletters out there that provide insights beyond what you'll find in academic journals. Authors like Nico McCarthy and Elliot Hershberg offer valuable perspectives on the industry. The field of TechBio is evolving rapidly, and there are incredible opportunities for those who are passionate about making a difference at the intersection of biology and technology. Reach out to Elise De Reus PhD on linkedin, and reach out to Cradle.Bio to learn more about their evolving services and offerings Thanks for reading Bits in Bio! title: Bits In Bio Weekly - September 3rd 2024
text: 📢 Highlights Helixfold, open source version of AlphaFold3, has been released by Baidu Noetik's Immunotherapy AI platform dev advances with $40M series A Emerging data shows that Semaglutide (aka Ozempic) may slow aging Call for Speakers! Bits in Bio Global Learning Mixer is seeking members  for lightning talks. Share your insights in a fun and engaging format. Interested? Please fill out this Google form to sign up! Not yet a member of our super awesome slack community of >7500? Join HERE 🤗 👀 In Case You Missed it .. HelixFold3, Baidu's open source replication of AlphaFold3 The PaddleHelix research team at Baidu Inc. has released their open-source replication of AlphaFold3 under a noncommercial license. They presented their results in a recent paper, reaching performance similar to those of AlphaFold3 on predicting the structures of small molecule ligands, nucleic acids (including DNA and RNA), and proteins. Below the different links to explore further: Arxiv: https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.16975 Website: https://paddlehelix.baidu.com Github: http://bit.ly/3Mt1YXt NOETIK Receives  $40M in Series A for Novel Immunotherapy AI Platform The 2 year old San Francisco based computational immunotherapy startup, NOETIK, has secured $40 million in Series A funding, led by Polaris Partners, with managing partner Amy Schulman set to join Noetik's board of directors, existing backers such as DCVC, Zetta Venture Partners, and Catalio Capital Management, and new investors, including Khosla Ventures, Wittington Ventures, and Breakout Ventures. The funding will accelerate the development of NOETIK's AI-driven precision cancer therapies, leveraging its Oncology Counterfactual Therapeutics Oracle (OCTO) platform for drug discovery, allegedly comprised of a new multimodal, transformer-based architecture that processes spatially aligned data from diverse modalities, such as multiplex protein staining, spatial gene expression, DNA sequencing, and histopathology images (e.g., H&E stained sections). A significant amount of capital raised will be allocated to the prodigious compute resources needed to operate the novel architecture. Noetik is actively seeking to establish partnerships with leading academic institutions, healthcare providers, and pharmaceutical companies. To lead these efforts, the company has onboarded former Recursion Chief Business Officer Shafique Virani, MD. Sanofi's BTK Inhibitor shows mixed results in key MS trials Sanofi's BTK inhibitor tolebrutinib had mixed results in key multiple sclerosis (MS) trials , showing success in slowing disability progression but falling short in two other measures. The Phase III studies were crucial in determining the drug's efficacy for MS treatment. While the positive outcome in disability progression is significant, the failures in other aspects could impact the drug's future prospects. The results highlight the ongoing challenges in developing effective treatments for complex conditions like MS. Recursion Shares Phase 2 Data of REC-994, First-in-Disease investigational treatment for symptomatic Cerebral Cavernous Malformation (CCM) Recursion Pharmaceuticals has shared its Phase 2 data for REC-994 , a drug designed to treat cerebral cavernous malformation (CCM), a rare brain condition. The treatment showed to be safe and tolerable in a mid-stage study, but with mixed results for efficacy. While this is a good milestone for Recursion and its AI-driven drug discovery approach to reach its primary safety and tolerability goal, the challenge will be scaling these results and translating them into approved therapies. Flagship Pioneering and Pfizer's deepen collaboration with Quotient Therapeutics Flagship Pioneering is accelerating its strategic partnership with Pfizer Inc, through the announcement of a new collaboration involving two research programs to identify potential novel targets in Cardiovascular and Renal diseases. This time, it aims to leverage incubated company Quotient Therapeutics’ platform in order to explore early discovery work in those two indications. Bayer and NExTRNA join forces to target lncRNAs in cancer Bayer has announced a strategic collaboration with NExTRNA Therapeutics to develop small molecules aimed at targeting long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) in oncology. This partnership is focused on exploring the potential of lncRNAs, which are involved in regulating gene expression and have significant implications in cancer development. The two companies will collaborate on two first-in-class small molecule programs with NextRNA having the possibility to receive up to $547 million including upfront, milestones and royalties. Vandria Raises $30.7M to Advance Mitophagy Therapy The Switzerland-based mitochondrial therapeutics  startup, Vandria has completed its Series A funding round, raising  $30.7 million led by Hevolution Foundation and Dolby Family Ventures. The investment will be used to advance its mitophagy-inducing drug, VNA-318, into Phase 1 clinical trials, targeting neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer's and Parkinson's. VNA-318 aims to restore cellular health by promoting the removal of damaged mitochondria, a key factor in the progression of these diseases. The global market for Alzheimer’s disease alone is projected to grow from $7.6 billion to >$10 billion by 2035, driven by aging populations and advances in detection. Can a diabetes drug Like Ozempic hold the key to slowing aging? A new study suggests that semaglutide, a drug used for Type 2 diabetes and obesity, might also slow aging by treating related conditions like heart failure, arthritis, Alzheimer's, and cancer. However, while mortality rates drop in overweight and diabetic patients, it's unclear if the drug delays aging or simply mitigates disease effects. The impact on healthy, non-overweight individuals remains untested, and potential muscle-wasting side effects raise concerns about its overall benefits. A trial on healthy older adults could help answer some of these questions. 🗓️ Upcoming BiB Events See our handy dandy Lu.ma event calendar HERE , please RSVP so folks can plan accordingly! title: Bits In Bio Weekly - August 27th 2024
text: 📢 Highlights . Bits in Bio Slack has been upgraded to the Pro Plan (for now). Thank you for donors today and please feel free to contribute to keep this community open. The discussion and reflection continues after Leash Bio’s competition. Community members discussing the favorite ML course and LIMs pricings Not yet a member of our super awesome slack community of >7500? Join HERE 🤗 👀 In Case You Missed it .. Nathan's Substack The Impact of AI/ML in Drug Discovery Isn't Where You Think It is Two recent articles on the impact of AI/ML and computation in biology caught my attention, one by Andrew Dunn with the provocative title “In a reality check for the field, AI underwhelms in Leash Bio's binding contest: 'No one did well'“ and the more concisely titled … Read more 2 years ago · 7 likes · Nathan C. Frey and Eric Dai In this new piece, Nathan C. Frey and Eric Dai explore the impact of AI/ML in drug discovery. They argue that while flashy AI contests like Leash Bio's binding competition highlight limitations in generalization, the true value of ML lies in automating critical, low-level decision-making tasks in biotech. These tasks, though less glamorous, are essential for making drug discovery faster and more efficient. The authors emphasize the need for a data-centric approach, integrating ML into proven drug discovery processes for meaningful advancements. Thanks for reading Bits in Bio! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work. Subscribe UCB Divests $680M in China Assets to Mubadala and CBC Group Belgian pharma giant UCB is shaking up its China strategy by selling parts of its business, including neurology and allergy drugs like Keppra and Zyrtec, to Mubadala and CBC Group for $680 million . While UCB offloads these mature products, it’s doubling down on future-focused areas like immunology and rare diseases. Mubadala and CBC are eyeing this acquisition to dominate the CNS biopharma landscape in China. Watch for this deal to close by the end of 2024. Bicara Therapeutics and Zenas BioPharma, two antibody startups file for IPOs amid summer slump Bicara Therapeutics and Zenas BioPharma have filed for initial public offerings , testing the waters amid sluggish markets. Bicara, a Boston-based cancer drugmaker, is advancing ficerafusp alfa, a bifunctional antibody in Phase 1 trials for head and neck cancer. Meanwhile, Zenas is developing obexelimab for immune diseases, with its Phase 3 program in immunoglobulin G4-related disease attracting attention. Neither company has set a price range for their shares yet. Elektrofi Raises $112M Series C to Simplify Biologics Delivery Elektrofi, a biotech startup focused on improving biologics delivery, has raised $112 million in a Series C funding round . This will support the scale of its platform and advance its partnerships with major pharmaceutical companies, helping to streamline the delivery of complex therapies and improve patient convenience. The company specializes in formulating high-concentration biologics for easier subcutaneous delivery, aiming to reduce the need for intravenous administration. Moderna Secures FDA Approval for Updated COVID-19 Vaccine Targeting KP.2 Variant Moderna has announced that its updated Spikevax COVID-19 vaccine, targeting the KP.2 variant, has received FDA approval for individuals aged 12 and older, and Emergency Use Authorization for children as young as 6 months. This update reflects Moderna's commitment to addressing evolving health threats with their mRNA technology. As the CEO, Stéphane Bancel, emphasizes, staying up to date with vaccinations remains crucial, especially with flu season approaching. The new formula is expected to be available soon in the US, providing timely protection against the latest variant. Johnson & Johnson Expands Cardiovascular Innovations with V-Wave Acquisition In a strategic move to boost its cardiovascular portfolio, Johnson & Johnson announced the acquisition of V-Wave , a pioneer in heart failure treatments, for $600 million upfront, with possible milestone payments up to $1.1 billion. V-Wave's Ventura® Interatrial Shunt, a minimally invasive device targeting heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, represents a significant advancement in addressing unmet medical needs. This acquisition underscores J&J’s commitment to advancing innovative solutions in high-impact healthcare areas, with the deal expected to close by the end of 2024. Bayer Cuts 150 Jobs at Basel Consumer Health HQ Amid Global Restructuring Bayer is cutting 150 jobs at its consumer health headquarters in Basel, Switzerland , as part of a global restructuring effort under CEO Bill Anderson. The layoffs, affecting the consumer health division and administrative roles, are part of a broader plan to streamline operations and reduce costs. While 40 new jobs will be created in Basel, the company aims to save €500 million this year and €2 billion by 2026 through these cuts, which are part of a larger reduction of 3,200 jobs globally in 2024. AlphaFold predictions of alternative conformations are driven by structure memorization This new study published by researchers of NIH and NCBI discusses how AlphaFold struggles with predicting alternative protein conformations, particularly for fold-switching proteins. While AlphaFold excels at predicting the most likely conformation, it accurately predicts fold switching in only 35% of cases and just 14% for proteins outside its training set. The study therefore suggests that these models often rely on memorized data rather than true structural and physical insights. To improve on this task, the authors of Umol published a new piece introducing a neural network called Cfold , which was trained on a carefully selected dataset split from the Protein Data Bank (PDB). Cfold shows promising results, successfully predicting over 50% of alternative protein structures with high accuracy. Predicting alternative conformations is crucial for understanding protein function, making this an important area of research. 🗓️ Upcoming BiB Events See our handy dandy Lu.ma event calendar HERE , please RSVP so folks can plan accordingly! title: Bits In Bio Weekly - August 20th 2024
text: 📢 Highlights . Sakana AI reveals 'The AI Scientist’ to augment the creativity of research Roche looks to spinout health data commoditization leader, Flatiron Health Leash Bio's recent contest has shown limitations in data-sparse bioAI Not yet a member of our super awesome slack community of >7500? Join HERE 🤗 👀 In Case You Missed it .. Sakana AI Introduces Digital Scientist A digital scientist platform has been unveiled by Sakana AI, cofounded by Llion Jones, one of the original authors of the legendary and transformer paper presciently named "Attention is all you need" , has been unveiled. The AI scientist automates research processes by generating hypotheses, designing experiments, and analyzing data. The platform is designed to work alongside human researchers to accelerate scientific discovery. It has shown promising results in pilot studies, offering significant time savings while maintaining rigorous scientific standards. Sakana's approach could redefine how research is conducted in both academia and industry. This effort competes with others in the space, including those by Bay-area based Future House and others. Roche May Sell Flatiron Health Due to Strategic Challenges Consideration is being given by Roche to divest its cancer data subsidiary, Flatiron Health, which it purchased for $1.9 billion in 2018. Challenges stem from Roche's ownership limiting Flatiron's ability to collaborate with other pharmaceutical firms, affecting two-thirds of its revenue from anonymized cancer data sales. This shift in strategic value has raised questions about Flatiron's future under Roche's umbrella, despite its continued success in academic and healthcare partnerships. This comes as a shock to many, with the types of data that Flatiron trafficked in considered to be incredibly valuable. Nonetheless, with new market entrants, it is becoming a race to the bottom. AI Models Struggle in Leash Bio's Protein Binding Contest Current AI models face significant limitations in predicting protein binding for novel molecules, as revealed by Leash Bio's recent Kaggle contest. Despite the hype, t he competition showcased that AI struggles with generalization in complex biochemical interactions . Leash Bio's CEO Ian Quigley noted that progress will likely require datasets orders of magnitude larger than those available today, similar to advances seen in chess and protein folding AI. It is important to note that participation in this challenge entailed certain limitations, such as hybrid methods using molecular dynamics and atomistic simulations that are often used to overcome the problems competitors encountered. Halda Therapeutics Secures $126M for Cancer Therapy Development A Series B extension has raised $126 million for Halda Therapeutics , bringing total funding to $202 million for its RIPTAC cancer therapies. The funds will be used to advance the development of HLD-0915, a treatment targeting metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer. The lead candidate is expected to enter Phase 1 trials in 2025. Absci and MSK Partner for AI-Powered Cancer Therapeutics A partnership has been formed between Absci Corporation and Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center (MSK) to develop up to six novel cancer drugs using generative AI. By combining Absci's AI platform with MSK's expertise, the partnership targets preclinical breakthroughs by the end of 2024. The collaboration represents a significant leap in AI-driven oncology research, with ambitions to accelerate the development of life-saving treatments. Riverlane Secures $75M to Advance Quantum Error Correction A Series C funding round has raised $75 million for Riverlane to further develop its Deltaflow quantum error correction technology. The platform integrates QEC chips and software to correct billions of qubit errors per second, addressing a critical bottleneck in quantum computing. This funding round highlights the increasing demand for robust quantum error correction as the industry scales. 23andMe Pivots to Telehealth and GLP-1 Prescriptions The pioneering genetic testing company is shifting its focus from drug discovery to the rapidly growing GLP-1 market , launching a telehealth weight loss program that prescribes these medications. 23andMe is also conducting a large-scale genetic study to personalize GLP-1 treatments based on genetic responses. This strategic realignment follows the board's rejection of CEO Anne Wojcicki's plan to take the company private amidst pricing pressures and other challenges, with 23&me stock trading below 40 cents a share, a 96% reduction of value over 5 years. Recursion Launches World's First Neuroscience Phenomap "Neuromap," The first comprehensive map of the cell phenotypes (hence 'phenomap') encountered in neuroscience has been created by Recursion in collaboration with Roche and Genentech. This cutting-edge resource was built using over 1 trillion hiPSC-derived neuronal cells and state-of-the-art AI techniques. It has led to a $30 million milestone payment from the partners, who aim to leverage this tool for groundbreaking neuroscience research and drug discovery. Neuromap represents a significant advancement in understanding the complexities of the human brain. OpenFold Consortium Adds Six New Members for Drug Discovery Six new members have joined the OpenFold Consortium—Biogen, Congruence Therapeutics, POLARISqb, Psivant Therapeutics, SandboxAQ, and Astex Pharmaceuticals. These new collaborators enhance OpenFold's open-source AI tools for drug discovery , strengthening the consortium's capacity to accelerate therapeutic innovation. The expansion underscores the growing interest in collaborative, open-source platforms for advancing drug development. Persist AI and Nivagen Streamline Long-Acting Injectable Manufacturing An AI-driven manufacturing process for long-acting injectables is being developed through a partnership between Persist AI and Nivagen Pharmaceuticals. These formulations are designed to improve patient adherence by providing sustained drug release . The collaboration aims to simplify production and expedite regulatory approvals, potentially revolutionizing how these treatments are brought to market. Pfizer Appoints New Chief AI Officer for Digital Innovation Berta Rodriguez-Hervas has been appointed as Pfizer's new Chief AI and Analytics Officer. Rodriguez-Hervas brings extensive experience from automotive manufacturer  Stellantis and GPU leader NVIDIA , where she focused on AI and machine learning operations. She will co-chair Pfizer's AI Council and lead efforts to integrate AI into drug discovery, clinical development, and operational efficiency. Biomatter Raises €6.5M to Revolutionize Enzyme Design with AI Seed funding of €6.5 million has been secured by Biomatter to advance its Intelligent Architecture™ platform, which uses generative AI to design custom enzymes from scratch. The technology has applications across biomanufacturing, agriculture, and life sciences. Already in use by industry leaders like Thermo Fisher and BASF, Biomatter's platform aims to cut development times for next-generation enzymes. Landmark Biodiversity Agreement Set by Basecamp Research and Cameroon A pioneering access and benefit-sharing partnership for genetic data has been launched by Basecamp Research and the government of Cameroon . This agreement makes Cameroon the first Central African country to implement such a deal, aimed at promoting fair compensation for the use of digital sequence information. The partnership sets a precedent ahead of COP 16, the global biodiversity summit. McGill Breakthrough in Stem Cell Therapy Mechanic s A novel technique for controlling stem cell differentiation has been developed by McGill University researchers, involving mechanically altering the cells' nuclei. This method allows precise transformation into specific cell types, such as bone or fat cells, with promising applications in regenerative medicine. While initial uses may focus on bone regeneration, clinical applications could take a decade or more to develop. ThermoFace Pioneers Non-Invasive Health Diagnostics Leveraging a wealth of health record integrated facial recognition data and technology, China's Peking University researchers have developed " ThermoFace," a diagnostic tool utilizing thermal facial imaging to assess biological age and predict conditions like diabetes and hypertension. ThermoFace offers a rapid, cost-effective approach to early disease detection and monitoring. This innovation could revolutionize how individuals monitor their health, with early trials showing accuracy in predicting metabolic diseases. The technology highlights the potential of non-invasive diagnostics, with surprising levels of insight. Blackrock Neurotech Restores Voice to ALS Patient via BCI A breakthrough in neurological technology was achieved by Blackrock Neurotech, as an ALS patient regained speech through a brain-computer interface (BCI) . The BCI translates thoughts into speech at a rate of 62 words per minute. This advancement highlights the potential for BCIs to restore communication abilities in individuals with severe neurological conditions 🗓️ Upcoming BiB Events See our handy dandy Lu.ma event calendar HERE , please RSVP so folks can plan accordingly! title: Bits In Bio Weekly - August 6th 2024
text: 📢 Highlights Microsoft and Paige.AI open source cancer image AI foundation models VC activity in biopharma posted $7.6B in Q2, challenging industry concerns Accelerated FDA approval provided for first engineered cell therapy for tumors Not yet a member of our super awesome slack community of >7500? Join HERE 🤗 👀 In Case You Missed it .. Open-Sources AI Models for Cancer Research from Paige and Microsoft New york based Paige has open-sourced its foundational AI models, PRISM and Virchow . Developed with Microsoft Research, Virchow is the first million-slide AI model for cancer detection, utilizing a dataset of 1.5 million digitized pathology slides from Memorial Sloan Kettering (MSK).  PRISM, trained with an additional 587,000 whole slide images and 195,000 clinical reports, provides multi-modal insights for cancer diagnosis and treatment, generating diagnostic summaries. These models are now available to researchers and developers to advance oncology research, and are avilable on Hugging Face . Virchow demonstrated clinical-grade performance across 16 cancer types, including rare cancers. Out of character for legendarily close sourced Microsoft, this shift towards open source models follows on the heels of the the recent release of the most powerful open source LLM to date, Meta’s Llama3 409B . Biopharma VC Activity Surges in Q2  in Contrast to General Sentiment Venture capital investment in biopharma reached $7.6 billion across 107 deals in Q2 , indicating robust industry growth. Major rounds included Xaira Therapeutics' $1 billion and Formation Bio's $372 million. J.P. Morgan forecasts $28.2 billion in venture funding for 2024, driven by strong M&A activity and an expanding IPO market. The sector saw a significant increase in investment compared to the previous quarter, providing bullish undertones for the remainder of the year bolstered by impactful sector-specific AI deployments. Biologics and small molecules continue to attract the most popular investments and licensing deals. FDA Accelerates Approval for Adaptimmune's TECELRA Adaptimmune has r eceived FDA accelerated approval for the first engineered cell therapy for solid tumor, TECELRA (afamitresgene autoleucel). A seminal achievement in drug development and the culmination of over a decade of R&D from hundreds of talented researchers, Teclera has been specifically developed for advanced MAGE-A4+ synovial sarcoma in adults with certain HLA types. This marks the first new treatment for synovial sarcoma in over a decade, providing a crucial option for patients who have undergone prior chemotherapy. This approval highlights the importance of HLA diversity within patient samples for effective precision medicines, with a submission for full approval in synovial sarcoma as soon as next year. A detailed breakdown of Teclera’s  mechanism of action and the indication it targets , from Edwin Choy, MD, PhD, director of the Sarcoma Program at MGH, is available online. VenBio Secures $528M for Selective Biotech Investments San Francisco VC firm, venBio has closed its fifth fund at $528 million to support about a dozen biotech startups over the next three years. This follows successful IPOs and M&A exits, including companies like Alumis, Artiva, and RayzeBio. The firm aims to strategically invest in groundbreaking biotech ventures that have high potential for impact, with a capital allocation  strategy engineered for long term support and capitalization of its portfolio companies. In terms of clinical indications, VenBio has played their cards close to their vest with a publicly stated focus on diseases with significant unmet medical needs. GSK and Flagship Pioneering Partner to Develop New Medicines A new collaboration has been announced between GSK and Flagship Pioneering , the well known boston-based biotech venture studio. The partnership will center around drug development for an undisclosed portfolio of respiratory and immunological indications. The initial funding for this partnership is up to $150 million, with potential milestones reaching $720 million. This collaboration aims to leverage the strengths of both companies to address unmet medical needs. The partnership will initially focus on discovering and developing up to 10 novel medicines and vaccines. 23andMe Board Rejects Wojcicki’s Privatization Plan The board of 23andMe has rejected CEO Anne Wojcicki's plan to take the company private at 40 cents per share, valuing it at $230 million. The board seeks changes and expects Wojcicki's support for a revised plan to stabilize the company's future. Wojcicki owns nearly 110 million shares, giving her significant voting power. The board is now evaluating alternative strategic options to improve the company's performance. The embattled CEO has come under harsh scrutiny as the once-marquee organization has seen its stock decline -80% over the past year. Asimov Launches AI-Driven CHO Edge System One of the original innovators in computational synthetic biology, Asimov has introduced its AI-driven 4th generation Edge System for CHO cells (Chinese Hamster Ovaries), which purportedly guarantees titers of 5 g/L for IgG monoclonal antibodies. This advanced system includes optimizations for various biologic architectures, achieving a titer range of 5-11 g/L. The system aims to significantly enhance the efficiency of biologic production, offering a robust solution for cell line development. The provision of a client guarantee of achieving specific titer levels is a bold move with limited industry precedence, a fact that the innovative team at Asimov is well aware of. Given 8 years of excellent work, Asimov is well positioned to make good on its promise. Rejuvenate Biomed and SAS Develop AI Drug Repurposing Tool In a somewhat surprising move, the 50 year old business statistics company, SAS has partnered with Belgium-based Rejuvenate Biomedicine to create an AI-driven, disease-agnostic drug repurposing discovery tool on SAS Viya. This innovative collaboration aims to accelerate drug development for age-related diseases, leveraging AI to identify new uses for existing drugs. The platform is set to disrupt the field of drug repurposing, bringing battleship-scale firepower to a cottage industry. The tool will initially focus on identifying new therapeutic uses for drugs that have already been approved for other indications, which has proven a wellspring of untapped value featuring significantly abbreviate time-to-market. GRO Biosciences Raises $60M for Protein Therapies GRO Biosciences has secured $60 million in Series B financing to advance its ProGly-Uricase gout drug, designed to avoid immune response issues seen with existing treatments. The technology utilizes non-standard amino acids to enhance efficacy and reduce adverse reactions, a strategy also employed in the development of the now well-known weight loss drop, Ozempic. This funding will support the development of transformative protein therapies. 🗓️ Upcoming BiB Events See our handy dandy Lu.ma event calendar HERE , please RSVP so folks can plan accordingly! title: Bits In Bio Weekly - July 30th 2024
text: 📢 Highlights Gilead’s twice-yearly injectable for preventing HIV shows nearly 100% efficacy King's College and EMBL’s Gut Microbiome Atlas reveals >1000 new species The largest protein interaction dataset yet has been released. Inspiration? Tinder InSilico announces DORA - a dubiously ethical AI for authoring research papers Not yet a member of our super awesome slack community of >7000? Join HERE 🤗 👀 In Case You Missed it .. Gilead's Sunlenca Injection Achieves 100% HIV Prevention In a landmark study involving 5,000 women and girls in South Africa and Uganda, Gilead's Sunlenca twice-yearly injections demonstrated 100% effectiveness in preventing HIV . This deeply inspirational development represents one of the most significant breakthroughs in HIV prevention, further cementing Gilead as the potential global leader in providing long-term protection and HIV prophylaxis strategies. Gilead has been on a decades-long crusade against HIV/AIDS , with its potent medication redefining possibilities in public health. This study's results could lead to broader adoption of Sunlenca as a preventative measure, and herald a massive increase in general quality of life for some of the most under-served populations in the world. New Gut Microbiome Atlas Offers Comprehensive Insights Researchers at King's College London and EMBL-EBI have unveiled the most comprehensive Gut Microbiome Atlas to date . Profiling microbiomes from over 250,000 samples worldwide, this atlas provides unprecedented insights into global gut health. These findings reveal the complex interplay between microbiomes and health outcomes, advancing our understanding of the gut microbiome's role in disease and informing future therapeutic developments. The Atlas has already identified over 1,000 previously unknown bacterial species, while recognizing that the promiscuous nature of bacterial genetic material exchange makes their phylogeny dynamic to the point of resistance to categorization. The gut microbiome has demonstrated increasingly pervasive health implications. For example, gut α-synuclein has been shown to drive Parkinson's Disease. ‘Protein Tinder’ Provides 500x the Interaction Data for Real-Life Protein Evaluations On a light-hearted note, a collaborative data curation effort led by VantAI has created the largest dataset of protein-protein interactions to date. With up to 25 million trainable examples, it is >500x larger than previous datasets. PINDER (protein + Tinder, or 'Protein INteraction Dataset and Evaluation Resource', depending on who you ask) provides training and evaluation data for producing and quantitatively assessing protein interaction and docking AI tools. The technique used to produce PINDER is allegedly similar to the well-known dating app. Unsurprisingly, the PINDER benchmark reveals that the performance of existing protein docking models, such as Alphafold Multimer, are highly overestimated when evaluated on the type of leaky test sets that are seen in actual lab settings. Curiously, the idea of 'swipe-based' molecular matching is not new. InSilico Medicine has used a similar approach for medicinal chemistry applications, and it was originally advanced by an exceptionally creative professor at the University of Toronto . Flagship Pioneering Launches AI Biotech with $50M Funding, Following Own $3.6B Raise Abiologics , a new biologics company from the biotech venture creation company Flagship, has launched with $50 million in funding. Abiologics will develop synthetic proteins called Synteins, designed to improve drug stability, enable oral dosing, and enhance cell penetration. This launch follows Flagship's recent $3.6Bn Fund VIII raise , aimed at supporting 25 new companies in human health, sustainability, and AI. The $3.6 billion funding includes strategic partnerships with Pfizer, Samsung, and Thermo Fisher, increasing Flagship's capital pool to $10.9 billion. Additionally, Flagship is expanding globally with new hubs in London and Singapore to foster international collaborations and talent acquisition. The Synteins technology has shown a 50% increase in bioavailability in preliminary studies, indicating its potential to reduce manufacturing costs while reducing off-target effects and adverse medical events. Paige and Microsoft Unveil Virchow Model for Cancer Detection A study in Nature Medicine has provided a showcase for a collaboration between Paige, an image-based diagnostics software provider, and Microsoft on Virchow , the first million-slide foundation model for cancer detection. This AI-driven model demonstrated clinical-grade performance across 16 cancer types, including rare cancers, by accurately identifying complex cancer patterns. Virchow's success signifies a major advancement in cancer diagnostics, leveraging large-scale AI models to improve detection and treatment outcomes. The model has shown a 95% accuracy rate in identifying malignant cells, meeting or exceeding most current model offerings. This development is another significant outcome of the Microsoft-Paige alliance, following t heir joint announcement of PRISM, a foundation model developed for diagnostic slide image analysis. Insilico Medicine Introduces Dubiously Ethical 'DORA' AI to Write your Research Papers Always the iconoclast, Insilico Medicine has introduced DORA , an AI assistant designed to help researchers draft medical research papers. As AI continues to penetrate the day-to-day lives of medical researchers, new conversations on the definition of authenticity and the very definition of authorship will need to be had. Allegedly targeting non-native speakers and early career professionals, DORA promises a reduction in drafting time by 40%. Though upside is foreseeable, this development will likely contribute to an evolving crisis. Peer-reviewed science may become polluted with low-quality and occasionally made-up papers produced by pressured academics facing the infamous 'publish or perish' dynamics known throughout the industry all too well. The 'paper mill' business has been a profitable one. Nature estimates over 400,000 articles have been produced using such intellectually dishonest approaches, with many featuring sham science and fabricated data. The development of AI systems for this very use case was forewarned by multiple agencies and organizations, with cross-industry consortia convening around detection methods and associated accountability systems. Touchlight Licenses Enzymatic DNA Technology to GSK London-Based Touchlight has licensed its enzymatic doggybone DNA (dbDNA™) technology to GSK , though details on the deal have not been disclosed. This agreement will enable the rapid and scalable GMP production of DNA templates for mRNA-based products, supporting GSK's development pipeline. The partnership aims to enhance GSK's mRNA research and development capabilities, thereby bolstering its emerging RNA vaccine business underway with Curevac. The dbDNA™ technology can produce DNA templates up to 10 times faster than traditional methods. Nerio Therapeutics Acquired for $1.3B  by Boehringer Ingelheim Boehringer Ingelheim announced the $1.3B acquisition of California-based Nerio Therapeutics . Nerio's small molecules inhibit the protein tyrosine phosphatases N1 and N2 (PTPN1 and PTPN2), which act as immune checkpoints and metabolic modulators. Through this mechanism, Nerio's First-in-Class therapeutic inhibition of PTPN1/2 can activate the immune system to fight cancer cells across diverse tumor types. 🗓️ Upcoming BiB Events See our handy dandy Lu.ma event calendar HERE , please RSVP so folks can plan accordingly!
